speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Okay so — and Brian, let me know if the technical depth here is useful for you or if you want me to translate as I go — basically what we're looking at is a cascading failure in the event processing pipeline. The short version is we had a single point of failure in the event ingestion layer, and when that node went down, there was no fallback. So events just started backing up and then the whole downstream monitoring stack lost visibility.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Yeah no that's — that's helpful context actually. My team has been getting hammered with tickets since Saturday morning and I want to make sure I understand what I'm telling customers, so.

sentence: As of this morning we're at a hundred and twelve open cases specifically tied to the Detect outage. About thirty of those are priority one or two accounts. We've got a handful of customers that are, uh, pretty vocal about it.

sentence: Okay. That's — yeah, that tracks with what I've been seeing from the account side. Raj, continuing on the root cause — what's the fix look like?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: So the fix is two-pronged. The immediate thing we're doing — and this is already in progress, the team started on it yesterday — is deploying redundant processing nodes. So instead of relying on a single ingestion point, we're moving to an active-active configuration. That should eliminate the single point of failure entirely.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: And the second piece?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: The second piece is implementing a circuit breaker pattern on the pipeline. So essentially, if one component starts degrading, the circuit breaker trips before it can take down the whole chain. It's a more resilient architecture overall. We should have had this from the start, honestly, but — yeah.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: I appreciate you saying that. I think — and I want to be candid here — I think there's going to be a postmortem conversation about how this got built the way it did, but I want to table that for now and focus on the remediation. What's the timeline looking like for full restoration?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Current estimate is we'll have the redundant nodes live by end of day today. The circuit breaker implementation is a little more involved — that's probably forty-eight hours from now, so, Wednesday evening at the earliest. But the monitoring visibility issue, the core thing customers are experiencing, that should be resolved today.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Okay so when you say resolved today — are we talking full visibility restored across all tenants, or is it phased?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: It'll be phased. We're going to roll it out starting with, uh, our larger enterprise tenants first and then work down. Realistically all tenants should have visibility restored by tomorrow morning.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Okay. That's a little frustrating because I've been telling customers we're targeting end of today. Can we — is there any way to compress that timeline or is tomorrow morning pretty firm?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Brian, I get it, but I'd rather give customers a conservative timeline and hit it than promise end of day and miss. We've already got a credibility issue here.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Yeah, no, that's fair. That's fair. I just — I need to go back and update a lot of those tickets and some of these customers are going to push back.

sentence: I can help draft the updated customer communication if that's useful, Brian. I think we should have something that acknowledges the delay, explains the phased rollout without getting too deep in the weeds technically, and gives them the concrete timeline.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Yeah, that would actually be really helpful. My team is stretched pretty thin right now.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Raj, on the six hours of no visibility — I want to make sure I understand this correctly. During that window, customers had zero threat monitoring data? Like nothing was coming through?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Correct. Events were being ingested at the edge but they were just... queuing up and not processing. So from a customer-facing dashboard perspective, it looked like nothing was happening. No alerts, no detections, nothing. Which is — yeah, that's bad.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: And we're confident that there were no actual threats during that window that went undetected? Or is that — can we even say that?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: That's a really important question and the honest answer is we've been doing retroactive processing of the queued events and so far we haven't identified any threats that were active during that window, but I want to be careful about saying definitively that nothing happened. We're still working through the backlog.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Okay so we cannot make any affirmative statements to customers about security posture during that window. Got it. Brian, make sure your team is not — if anyone's asking that question, the answer is we're doing a full retroactive analysis and we'll communicate findings.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Understood. Yeah, I'll make sure that's clear in the team briefing. We've had a few customers ask exactly that and I've been kind of hedging, so it's good to have a cleaner answer.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Raj, what's the ETA on completing that retroactive processing? I want to know when we can close the loop on that.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: We're probably looking at another twenty-four hours to fully process and review the backlog. I'll have a summary report by Wednesday that we can share internally, and then we can figure out what we communicate to customers from there.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Alright. Let's talk about the postmortem briefly — not to get into the full discussion, but I want to align on the process. Are we thinking internal postmortem first and then a customer-facing summary, or are we doing those in parallel?

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: I'd want to do the internal one first. We need to have our story straight before we put anything external out. I'm thinking we do a full engineering postmortem by end of this week, and then next week we can work on a customer-facing incident report.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: That timeline works for me. Brian, from a support perspective, is there anything you need for the postmortem process? Like data from the ticket queue or customer impact analysis?

speaker: Megan Lawson

speaker: Raj Kapoor

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Yeah, that matches what I've got. I'll start updating tickets this afternoon once Megan sends over draft language.

speaker: Sarah Chen

speaker: Gregory Fisk
sentence: Right, well, that's — I mean, that's a process thing we probably should have caught on our end too, so I'm not going to sit here and say it's entirely on Aegis. But the question now is what do we do about the invoice.

sentence: Absolutely, and that's a reasonable question. speaker: Gregory Fisk
sentence: Okay. And what's the typical turnaround on something like that?

sentence: Usually 5 to 7 business days for the review, and then if a credit or adjustment is approved it would appear on your next billing cycle. sentence: Yeah, there's a bulk deprovision option in the Aegis Identity admin console — if you go to User Management and then Provisioning, you can filter by account status, creation date, and there's also a tag-based filter if any of those test accounts were tagged during setup. speaker: Gregory Fisk

sentence: That's exactly it — I'll also send you a short prep document that explains what to expect from the baseline report so you and Priya aren't going in cold.

sentence: Perfect — honestly this call was really reassuring, I feel like we actually have a path forward now, which is not how I felt last week.

speaker: Daniel Okafor
sentence: That's exactly what we want to hear — alright Thomas, we'll let you get back to it, talk soon.

speaker: Thomas Hargrove
sentence: Sounds great, thanks guys — really looking forward to it.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Of course. Why don't you go first actually — what's on your mind?

sentence: Yeah, and I completely understand. I was going to bring that up myself. That was March 10th through roughly the 18th, the event processing issue — six hours of no visibility on threat monitoring.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Completely fair. And I don't want to just gloss over it with a, you know, 'here's what we did to fix it' kind of answer if you have more to say about the impact on your side.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: I mean, we didn't have an incident during that window, which is lucky, but it surfaced a gap in our own contingency planning too, so it wasn't all on Aegis. It just... it raised questions internally about the reliability of the platform.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: That's really helpful context, and I appreciate you being candid about it. On our end, the root cause was a single point of failure in the event ingestion pipeline — it was a cascading failure from there. Engineering has since deployed redundant processing nodes and implemented a circuit breaker pattern, so the architecture is fundamentally different now in that layer.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Okay. Is there documentation on that? Like, something I can actually share with my team rather than just taking your word for it?

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Yes, absolutely. We put out a full post-incident review — I'll send that over after the call. It goes into the root cause analysis, the remediation steps, and the testing we did before re-deploying.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Good. That'll help. My team wants to see that it's not just a band-aid.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Understood. And honestly if there are follow-up questions after your team reviews it, I can also set up time with one of our solution architects to walk through the technical details.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: That might be worth doing, yeah. Let me see what the team thinks after they read the doc. Okay, so that's the main concern. What else — you said you wanted to cover the renewal specifically?

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Right. So your current contract runs through end of June, so we're about... eleven weeks out. I wanted to start the conversation early so we're not scrambling in June.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Smart. Yeah. What are you looking at — is pricing changing?

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: So, there is a standard escalator built into the contract — I believe it's three percent — and that would apply. But I also want to understand what your scope looks like going into next year before we lock anything in, because if you're expanding, that changes the conversation.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Mm. Yeah, so we're planning to add about forty more cloud workloads through Q3 as we finish migrating the last of our on-prem stuff. So the footprint is growing.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Okay, that's good to know. Are those workloads going to be on CloudPrime primarily, or is it a mix?

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Mix. Mostly CloudPrime, some Azure — we've got about a 70/30 split right now and I think it stays roughly there.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Got it. That's helpful. So I'll put together some options — probably a base renewal at your current tier, and then a growth option that prices in that expanded workload count. I can model out what makes sense financially for both sides.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: That'd be useful. And, um, I do want to flag — we're not exclusively evaluating Aegis. I have procurement pushing me to get at least one competitive quote, so... I want to be transparent about that.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: I appreciate you being upfront. Who are you looking at, if you don't mind me asking?

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Probably SentinelShield, just because they've been pretty aggressive in our space lately. I don't have a strong pull toward them but procurement wants the comparison.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Fair enough. I'll make sure our proposal is competitive on value, not just price. And I'm happy to do a side-by-side if that would be helpful for your internal review.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Maybe. Let me see what SentinelShield comes back with first.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Of course. Just know the offer is there. Okay, I also wanted to touch on Comply v2 — you're currently on Comply, right? The new version just went GA on April 7th.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Oh yeah, I saw the release notes. The on-demand reporting — we've actually already poked around in it a little.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Oh great, any early impressions?

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: It's... decent. The SOC 2 report generation is noticeably faster than v1. We haven't dug into the HIPAA or ISO stuff yet because that's not really our priority, but the multi-framework view is something we'll probably use for PCI.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: That's good to hear on the SOC 2 side — that's been one of the most common things customers come to us about. And yeah, PCI DSS coverage in v2 is pretty robust, so I'd encourage you to test that whenever you get a chance.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: We will. Our next PCI audit cycle is Q4 so I want to have that dialed in before then.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Perfect timing then. I can connect you with our compliance specialist if you want a guided walkthrough before you're in the thick of audit prep.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Yeah, actually, that would be useful. Maybe in June or so.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: I'll make a note of that. Okay, so to recap what I'm taking away — I'll send the post-incident review for the Detect outage, I'll put together two renewal proposal options with pricing that reflects the expanded workload, and I'll pencil in a compliance walkthrough for sometime in June. speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: I think so. And if we decide to loop in the solutions architect on the Detect stuff, I'll reach out through you.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Absolutely, just ping me and I'll coordinate. What's a good turnaround for the proposal — does two weeks work, so around the 24th?

speaker: Lauren Bishop

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Perfect. Alright, I'll let you get back to your Q2 planning chaos. Thanks again for the time, Lauren.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Thanks Lisa. Talk soon.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Alright, I think we've got everyone on. Let me just do a quick roll call — Tyler, Raj, Ravi, Brian, Mike — okay yeah looks like we're all here.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Yep, I'm here.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: Here.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Yeah, good to go.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Here, sorry I'm like thirty seconds late, had a thing.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: No worries. Okay so, the reason I pulled everyone together today — as you all know we've got our SOC 2 Type II audit window coming up, and I want to make sure we're not scrambling at the last minute. The auditors are going to want evidence going back several months so we need to be organized now.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Do we have the audit window confirmed? Last I heard it was still tentative.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Yeah, it got confirmed last week. We're looking at a roughly ninety-day observation period, starting March first and running through end of May. Auditors will do their fieldwork in June.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Okay, so we've got like three days before the observation period actually kicks off. That's not a ton of runway.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Right, which is why we're doing this now. I want to go through the key control areas and figure out who owns what. Tyler, I was going to start with you on the access control and change management side.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Sure. So for access control, I think we're in pretty decent shape. We've got access reviews running quarterly, last one was — uh, I want to say January fifteenth? I'd have to double-check the exact date. And we're logging provisioning and deprovisioning events through Aegis Identity.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Are those logs being retained for the right duration? SOC 2 auditors are going to want to see at minimum a year of retention depending on the trust service criteria.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: That's actually a good question. Ravi, you're closer to the logging infrastructure — what are we configured to right now?

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: So right now I believe we're at thirteen months retention for the identity and access logs specifically. I set that up back in Q3. For the broader system logs it's a bit more varied — some are at ninety days, some at a year. I can pull together a full inventory this week if that's helpful.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Yeah, please do that. And flag anything that's under a year, we should probably normalize that before March first if we can.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: I can have something by Wednesday.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Perfect. Okay, change management — Tyler, where are we on that?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: So we've got the change advisory board process documented, and most production deploys are going through the proper approval workflow in Jira. The one gap I'm a little worried about is the hotfix process — we've had a few cases where engineers pushed directly to prod without a formal ticket and then backdated the documentation.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah, that's going to be a problem. Auditors will look at commit history and deployment logs and they'll see the timing doesn't line up.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: It happened, I think, twice in the past quarter. Both times it was legitimate emergencies but still — the process wasn't followed.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: We need to tighten that up going forward during the observation period. Can we add a gate in the CI/CD pipeline so that anything touching production requires a ticket reference? Like, a hard gate, not just a reminder.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: Yeah that's doable. I can build that out. It'll probably take me a day or two, I'd want to test it in staging first so we don't break the deployment pipeline.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Good. Let's get that in before March first. Brian, I want to pull you in here — from a support perspective, do you have visibility into how incidents are being tracked and escalated? That's going to come up under the availability trust service criteria.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Yeah so we use PagerDuty for alerting and Zendesk for the customer-facing ticket side. I'd say the process is reasonably well documented. The thing I want to flag is that we don't always have great linkage between a customer ticket and the underlying incident record. Like, if a customer reports something and we tie it to a system incident, that connection isn't always formally captured.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Is that a manual process right now or is there supposed to be an integration?

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: It's supposed to be integrated but honestly it's kind of broken. The Zendesk to PagerDuty link works maybe sixty percent of the time. My team has been working around it manually.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Okay, that's a gap we need to address. Even if the auditors don't specifically flag the integration, if they pull a sample of incidents and can't trace them end to end, that's going to be a finding.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: I agree. I've been meaning to get someone to look at that integration for like two months, it just keeps getting deprioritized.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Mike, can you take a look at the Zendesk PagerDuty integration and see what's going on? I don't want to put more on Ravi's plate.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah, I can take that. I'll coordinate with Brian to understand what's actually failing. My guess is it's probably an API auth token that expired or something like that, but I won't know until I look.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: That would actually make sense, we rotated some credentials in December.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah, I'll take a look. Should be fixable pretty quickly if that's the case.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Okay good. Let me pivot to monitoring and threat detection. We use Aegis Detect for that internally — Ravi, what's the state of our alerting coverage? Are we logging the right events?

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: So coverage is pretty good overall. We're monitoring login anomalies, privilege escalations, API rate limit breaches, network egress outliers — basically the standard playbook. One thing I've been meaning to raise is that we have some alert fatigue going on. The tuning hasn't been updated in a while and we're getting a lot of low-signal alerts that people are starting to ignore.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: That's actually a risk from an audit perspective too. If auditors see a bunch of unacknowledged alerts in the system that's going to raise questions about whether we're actually reviewing them.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: Exactly. I'd like to do a tuning pass before the observation period starts. Maybe a half-day exercise where we go through the alert configs and suppress or recategorize the noisy ones.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Let's schedule that. Who needs to be in that session?

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: Me, Tyler probably, and it might be worth having Brian there since some of the alerts feed into the support escalation queue.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Works for me. Let's try to do that Tuesday or Wednesday this week.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Okay I'll send a calendar invite. Now — vendor management and third-party risk. This is one that I feel like we always underinvest in until an auditor asks about it. Do we have current SOC 2 reports for our critical vendors?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Uh, I know we have CloudPrime's compliance reports and we have something for Zendesk but it might be out of date. I'd have to check.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah that's something we should inventory. I can take a first pass at it — I'll pull together a list of our critical third-party vendors and check what compliance documentation we have on file versus what's missing.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: That would be great. We also need to make sure we have BAAs in place for anything that touches customer data. I know that's more of a legal thing but someone needs to chase it down.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: I can loop in legal and make sure those are accounted for. That's probably a quick check.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Good. Okay, let me just summarize what we've got so far in terms of action items. Ravi, you're doing the log retention inventory by Wednesday and the CI/CD gate for production deployments before March first. Mike, you're looking at the Zendesk PagerDuty integration and taking a pass at the vendor compliance documentation. Tyler, you're following up with legal on BAAs and keeping the access review records organized. Brian, you're participating in the alert tuning session. Does that all sound right?

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: That tracks for me.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yep.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Good with me.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Same.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: One more thing I want to raise before we wrap up — employee security training. Auditors consistently ask about this. Do we have completion records for security awareness training for all staff?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: We ran a training in November and I believe we got pretty close to a hundred percent completion but there were a handful of people who didn't finish it. I'm not sure if we ever followed up.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: That handful is going to matter to an auditor. If there are any outstanding completions we need to chase those down and document that we followed up, even if we can't get everyone to retroactively complete it.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Agreed. Tyler, can you pull the completion report from our LMS and send it around? We can figure out who still needs to complete it and decide if we want to issue a new training cycle or just document the exceptions.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Yeah, I'll get that today.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Alright. I think we're in reasonable shape but there's clearly work to do before March first. Let's plan to do a quick check-in next Friday to see where things stand. I'll set up a shared tracker so everyone can update their items.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: That works. I'll keep an eye on the tracker.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Okay, I think that's everything for today. Thanks everyone, let's get this stuff done.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Hi, is this Kendra? This is David Kim calling from Aegis Cloud Security support, I have case number 6977 pulled up here.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Yes, hi David, thanks for calling back. I've been waiting on this — we submitted that ticket two days ago.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I completely understand, and I apologize for the delay getting back to you. I want to make sure we get this sorted out today. So you're seeing slow backup performance with Aegis Protect — can you walk me through what you're experiencing?

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Yeah, so — I mean, it's been terrible honestly. Our nightly backup jobs are taking, uh, somewhere between four and six hours to complete. They used to finish in under ninety minutes. This started maybe three weeks ago, and it's just... it's not getting better.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Four to six hours, okay, that's a significant regression. And this is consistent every night, or are there certain nights where it's worse than others?

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: It's pretty much every night. I mean, maybe slightly worse on weekends but honestly it's bad across the board. And look, we're a retail company — we have a lot of transaction data, inventory records, customer data — we can't have backup windows bleeding into our morning operations.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Right, yeah, that makes total sense and that's a real operational concern. Um, let me ask — what's the approximate data volume you're backing up each night? Do you have a rough figure?

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: It's around, uh, two to two and a half terabytes per night. That hasn't changed dramatically. I mean, we've grown a little but nothing that should cause this kind of slowdown.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Got it. And have you made any changes on your end around that time — network configuration, storage infrastructure, anything like that? Or was this kind of just one day it started being slow?

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Not that I'm aware of. I mean, I had my team look into it and we didn't push any major infrastructure changes around that time. So we're kind of pointing the finger at your platform, honestly.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I hear you, and I'm not here to deflect blame — I want to find the root cause wherever it is. Let me pull up your account and look at the job logs from the last couple weeks here.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Please do. And while you're at it, I want to flag — we're running incremental backups, not full backups, so the fact that it's taking this long is extra concerning to me.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Yeah, that's a really good point, incremental jobs should absolutely not be taking that long. Okay, I'm in your account now... I can see the job history here. Hmm. Okay so I'm seeing some things.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: What are you seeing? Because my team has been asking me for answers and I don't have any.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: So I can see that your backup jobs are completing, but the throughput rate has dropped significantly. You're running at roughly 120 to 140 megabytes per second, whereas historically your jobs were running closer to 400 or 500. That's the bottleneck.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Okay so something changed on your end then. Because we didn't touch anything.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: It could be a few things — I want to dig into this more before I point fingers anywhere. One thing I'm noticing is that your backup agent version — you're running 3.4.1, and we released 3.6.0 back in late January. There were some performance optimizations in that release, specifically around incremental change detection.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Wait — so you're saying there might be a performance issue in the version we're running and there's already a fix out?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I don't want to say definitively that's the cause yet, but it is worth flagging. There was a known issue in 3.4.x where under certain conditions the change block tracking mechanism could — um — essentially over-scan, meaning it's processing more data than it needs to.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: David, that sounds like exactly what's happening. Why weren't we notified about this? This feels like something we should have gotten an email about or... I don't know, something.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: That's fair feedback and I'm going to note that in the case. Customers on affected versions should have been flagged for that update and I'll look into why that didn't happen for your account. That's a process gap we need to close.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Yeah, I mean — look, I like Aegis, we've been customers for over a year and overall we've been happy. But this kind of thing is frustrating. We're a retail business, our data pipeline is critical, and three weeks of bad backups is not something I can just shrug off.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Absolutely, and I'm not asking you to shrug it off. Let's focus on getting you fixed first and then I'll make sure the right people on our side hear this feedback. So — upgrading to 3.6.0, that's the immediate step I'd recommend. Can your team do that tonight?

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: We can probably do it tonight or tomorrow night. What's involved in the upgrade? Is there any downtime, any risk?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: It's a rolling upgrade, so there's minimal impact. The agent restarts but the restart window is typically under two minutes. You'd want to do it outside your backup window obviously, but it shouldn't cause any data issues or anything like that.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Okay. And what if we do the upgrade and it's still slow? Then what?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: If you're still seeing the issue after the upgrade, the next thing I'd want to look at is your network egress path — specifically whether there's any throttling or rate limiting happening between your environment and our ingestion endpoints. I can also set you up with enhanced logging on the next backup run so we get more granular telemetry.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Okay, that's fine. I mean I'm hopeful the upgrade fixes it but I've been hopeful before with this and it hasn't gotten better on its own.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I get it. I don't want to just give you hope and disappear — here's what I'll do. I'm going to send you the upgrade instructions in writing right after this call, and I'll personally follow up with you on Monday morning to check on how the backup ran. I'll keep this case open until we confirm the performance is back to where it should be.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Monday morning works. But David, if we're still seeing the same numbers Monday, I need escalation. I can't keep going back to my team with 'we're looking into it.'

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Completely fair. If the upgrade doesn't resolve it and we don't have a clear answer by Monday, I'll escalate this to our senior engineering team and loop in your account manager. You have my word on that.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Alright. I'll hold you to that. Go ahead and send me those upgrade instructions and I'll get my team moving on it tonight.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Will do. I'll have that in your inbox within the next thirty minutes. And Kendra — again, I'm sorry this dragged on as long as it did. We'll get it resolved.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Appreciate it. Talk Monday.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Talk Monday. Thanks Kendra, take care.

sentence: Got it, I'm pulling those up right now. Give me just a second.

sentence: Sure. So the main one is — there's an overage charge on both invoices under the Aegis Detect module. And honestly our team wasn't totally aware that we had a usage cap on that tier.

sentence: Okay so it's not a massive number, but my finance team's concern is more just the principle of it — they didn't know the cap existed and they want to understand if this is going to keep happening.

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: That's a totally fair concern and honestly it's something we should have been more proactive about flagging. Maria, do you have visibility into the event volume trending for Atlas?

sentence: Yeah I do. So looking at the last six months, Wayne, your event volume has been gradually climbing — which honestly makes sense, you've got more endpoints connected to the platform than you did at contract start. speaker: Wayne Keller
sentence: Yeah, that's right, we brought the Dayton and Phoenix plants online for Detect coverage. I guess that would account for some of it.

sentence: Yeah that makes sense. I'd want to see that laid out in writing so I can show my finance team, but directionally it seems reasonable.

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: Absolutely, I'll put together a revised pricing summary that shows the current tier versus the 750 million tier with the cost comparison. I can have that to you by end of week.

speaker: Wayne Keller
sentence: That works. The other thing I wanted to ask about — and this is more forward-looking — we've been getting some pressure internally to get better compliance reporting in place. Specifically around SOC 2. Is that something Aegis handles?

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: Yes, that falls under Aegis Comply — you're currently not subscribed to that module but it's something we can definitely talk through. Maria, do you want to give a quick overview?

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Sure. So Aegis Comply is our compliance reporting and audit module. It supports multiple frameworks — SOC 2 is one of them, along with things like HIPAA and PCI DSS. It's designed to give you on-demand reporting rather than having to manually compile audit evidence.

speaker: Wayne Keller
sentence: Okay. Is that something that would be priced separately on top of the existing contract?

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: It is a separate add-on, yeah. We can include the Comply pricing in that summary doc I'm putting together so you have the full picture. Depending on the scope, there are also some bundle discounts available when you're renewing.

speaker: Wayne Keller
sentence: Sure, include it. I'm not committing to anything yet but I want to have the numbers in front of me when I talk to our CFO.

sentence: It's Blackridge Investments, B-L-A-C-K-R-I-D-G-E, account number BRI-04471, and I also already submitted a ticket, ticket number... uh, I have it here somewhere — it's AGS-229183.

sentence: Okay, and while we're waiting — I have to be honest with you, Marcus, and I want you to pass this up the chain — we have been evaluating SentinelShield for the past couple months as a potential alternative and right now, today, this is making that decision very easy.

speaker: Marcus Williams
sentence: And that is — look, I'm really sorry you're in that position, and something I can help with is making sure you have a full incident report with timeline, root cause, and remediation steps that you can bring to that board meeting, that's something we can absolutely provide.

sentence: That would be helpful, yes, I'll need that in writing, and I want our account executive looped in too because there are going to be SLA conversations that need to happen.

sentence: Yeah, we're already doing that, we've had two analysts manually reviewing CloudTrail for the last two hours — that's two people pulled off other work to compensate for your platform being down.

speaker: Marcus Williams
sentence: And that operational impact is going to be part of the SLA conversation, I'm documenting it.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Okay — do you have any update from engineering yet? Any ETA at all?

sentence: I'm checking the internal incident channel right now and... okay, engineering is saying they've isolated the failed node and are in the process of restarting the processing queue, they're estimating restoration within the next two hours, but I want to be careful about that number because they're still in active remediation.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Two more hours. So potentially five-plus hours total of no threat monitoring. I just — I want to make sure that is in writing somewhere because that is five hours of SLA breach and I will be referencing that.

speaker: Marcus Williams
sentence: It is documented, I'm timestamping everything in the ticket and I'll make sure the incident report captures the full outage window from 7:14 AM through restoration.

sentence: And what happens to events that were supposed to be ingested during the outage window — are those just lost? Or will they be backfilled?

sentence: Alright, I think we've got everyone — Sofia, you just joined right?

sentence: Yep, recording is on. Okay so — Q2 roadmap planning for Identity. I'm really excited about this one honestly, I think we have a genuinely strong quarter ahead of us.

sentence: Same, and I feel like Q1 kind of lit the fire a little bit? Like, the Comply v2 launch last week showed what we can do when we're really coordinated, and I want that energy for Identity.

sentence: Totally agree with that. Comply v2 was a good moment for the org. And honestly, Identity has been in a bit of a holding pattern, so I think customers are ready to see some movement from us.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Yeah, I've been looking at the support ticket volume and there's a pretty clear cluster around SSO configuration — specifically around just-in-time provisioning. Like, that's coming up again and again.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Right, and that's actually on my list as a Q2 priority. So let me just share my screen and walk through the draft roadmap so we're all looking at the same thing.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Perfect.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Okay so — three big themes for Q2. First is JIT provisioning and SCIM improvements, second is adaptive MFA policies, and third is what I'm calling the Identity audit trail, which ties into the Comply integration story.

speaker: Jordan Whitfield
sentence: Oh, I love that the audit trail piece connects to Comply. That feels like a really natural cross-module story and honestly something we should be talking about in sales materials too.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: A hundred percent, and that was actually a direct ask from — I think it was the Fortiguard Pro competitive deal we almost lost last quarter? The customer specifically said they wanted IAM events surfaced in their compliance reports.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Yeah I remember that one. That was painful. But it's good that it's shaping actual roadmap items rather than just becoming a post-deal retrospective that nobody reads.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Exactly. Okay so let's go a level deeper on each one. Mike, you've been thinking about the SCIM work — do you want to take JIT provisioning?

sentence: Sure, yeah. So what I'm proposing is we build out a proper attribute mapping UI, add support for group-based role assignment, and fix the SCIM endpoint inconsistencies that have been causing sync failures.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: The sync failures specifically — do we have a sense of scope there? Because I know Ananya you've been in the weeds on that.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Yeah so there are basically three distinct failure modes. One is race conditions when you have concurrent provisioning requests, two is the SCIM patch operation not handling partial updates correctly, and three is just some gnarly edge cases around deprovisioning when a user is in multiple groups. None of them are insurmountable but they're all kind of annoying in their own way.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: How long are we thinking for that piece — like, just a rough estimate at this point.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: If Mike and I take it together I'd say four to five weeks? We'd want to do it properly with good test coverage because the last thing we need is a reliability issue on top of everything the Detect outage already stirred up in terms of customer trust.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah that's a fair point. The March outage definitely put everyone a bit more on edge about stability. I think that's actually — it's a good forcing function for us to be really disciplined about testing this quarter.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Agreed. And I think the customers who were affected by Detect are going to be watching us closely, so any Identity work we ship needs to feel rock solid.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Okay so let's pencil in JIT and SCIM as weeks one through five roughly. Jordan, for the attribute mapping UI — have you had a chance to look at any of the existing flows? Because I know there's some legacy stuff in there.

speaker: Jordan Whitfield
sentence: I have, yeah, and honestly it's — it's not terrible but it's also not great. The configuration screens feel like they were designed for an engineer rather than an IT admin, which is probably accurate. I've started sketching out a redesigned attribute mapping flow that uses a more visual drag-and-drop approach and gives you inline validation so you can see if your mappings are going to work before you save.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Oh that inline validation piece is really smart, because right now you basically have to save, trigger a test login, see that it broke, go back and try to figure out what happened. It's a terrible loop.

speaker: Jordan Whitfield
sentence: Right, exactly! And it generates a disproportionate number of support tickets because people just — they don't know what they did wrong. So if we can surface that feedback in the moment it should meaningfully reduce support load too.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Love it. Okay, adaptive MFA policies — Sofia, this is yours I think.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Yeah so the vision here is moving away from static MFA rules — like, everyone in this role gets MFA — toward something that's context-aware. So you'd factor in things like login location, device posture, time of day, whether it's an unusual access pattern. And based on that you can either prompt for MFA, step up authentication, or in low-risk scenarios maybe let it through without friction.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: That's a big feature though, right? Like, are we scoping the full adaptive engine or is this a first iteration?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Definitely first iteration. I'm thinking we start with location and device posture as the two signal inputs, build the policy engine to be extensible so we can add more signals later, and ship that as a beta in Q2. Full GA with more signals would be Q3.

speaker: Jordan Whitfield
sentence: That's a really sensible scope. And I think shipping it as beta is smart — we can get real customer feedback on the policy configuration experience before we commit to the full design.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Yeah and honestly from a competitive standpoint this is overdue. SentinelShield has had something like this for a while and we keep getting asked about it in enterprise deals.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Ugh, yeah. Every time SentinelShield comes up in a deal it's like — okay, what's their talking point this time. But I do think our implementation can be more tightly integrated with the rest of the platform, which is a real differentiator.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: That's exactly the angle. Like, the fact that adaptive MFA signals can feed into the Identity audit trail and then surface in Comply reports — that's something SentinelShield can't do because they don't have the compliance layer.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Okay so then the audit trail — this is the one I'm most excited about personally. Can we talk through what the data model looks like? Because I want to make sure we're designing this in a way that Comply can actually consume it cleanly.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Yeah absolutely. So the idea is that every significant Identity event — login, logout, MFA challenge, provisioning change, role assignment, policy update — gets written to a structured event log with a consistent schema. And then we expose that via an internal API that Comply can query when it's generating reports.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: And the Comply team is on board with this? Like, have you guys talked to them?

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: I had a quick chat with Priya on the Comply team last week, yeah. She was really enthusiastic about it. Apparently identity access events are one of the biggest gaps in what they can currently include in SOC 2 reports, so this would actually unlock a lot for their customers too.

speaker: Jordan Whitfield
sentence: That's great to hear. I think cross-team stuff like this is where the platform story really shines and it's something we should be doing more of in general.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Agreed. Okay so — I think we have a pretty solid shape for Q2. JIT and SCIM improvements, adaptive MFA beta, Identity audit trail. Mike, do you want to take a first pass at breaking these down into epics in Jira before our next planning session?

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah I can do that by end of week. I'll also flag any dependencies I see on shared infrastructure so we can get those on the radar early.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Perfect. And Jordan — are you good to share the attribute mapping designs in Figma this week so the team can give feedback async?

speaker: Jordan Whitfield
sentence: Absolutely, I'll post the link in the Identity channel tomorrow morning. I'm really happy with where it's heading and I want everyone's eyes on it before I get too attached to any particular direction.

speaker: Alex Reyes
sentence: Ha, the designer's curse. Okay, I think that covers everything — honestly this was one of the more energizing planning sessions we've had in a while. I feel good about where we're headed.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Yeah, same. Q2 is going to be a good one.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Alright, thanks everyone. Talk soon.

sentence: I think that's a fair characterization and I'm not going to push back on that. We'll get your account manager involved and make sure that conversation happens. sentence: And Paula, I want to reiterate — this situation is not okay, and I'm not treating it as business as usual. You'll have a written update from me in two hours with the fix timeline, the backfill status, and a summary of the incident for your records. Does that work?

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: And apparently didn't fix the underlying architecture, because here we are. My CISO is now asking me to put together a competitive evaluation. Those were her words. Competitive evaluation. She specifically mentioned SentinelShield.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Okay. Grace, I — I appreciate you being direct with me about that, and I'm not going to panic and just throw discounts at you right now because I don't think that's what you need. What I'm hearing is that you need confidence that this won't happen again.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Confidence would be a start, yes. But honestly? I'm past the point of just needing reassurance. I need to see a concrete remediation plan, I need an SLA commitment that actually has teeth, and I need to understand why I should tell my CISO to cancel that evaluation.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Those are completely fair asks and I'm going to commit to each of them. Can I walk you through what I know the engineering team is putting in place?

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Please. Go ahead.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: So the immediate fix that's already been deployed — and this went in last night — is redundant processing nodes in the event ingestion layer, so there's no longer a single point of failure there. And they're also implementing what's called a circuit breaker pattern, which basically means if one component starts degrading, it gets isolated before it can cascade.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Okay. And why wasn't that in place before? I mean — I'm sorry, I don't mean to be harsh, but that sounds like table stakes for a platform that's supposed to be monitoring security threats in real time.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: You're not wrong. And I — I genuinely don't have a good answer for why that architecture decision was made the way it was. What I can tell you is that it's being corrected, and I want to get you on a call with our VP of Engineering directly so you can ask that question yourself and hear it from the person who's accountable.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Hmm. That's — actually, yeah, I would want that. I want to hear it from someone technical, not just from a relationship layer.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Absolutely, I'll make that happen. I'm thinking early next week — does Monday or Tuesday work for you and your CISO?

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: I'll have to check with her, but... tentatively, yes. I'll say yes for now.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Okay, good, I'm putting that in my notes right now. And Grace, on the SLA question — I want to revisit your current agreement with us because I believe you're on our standard tier, and frankly for an organization of Northstar's size and your compliance requirements, I think we should be looking at what our enterprise SLA commitments look like.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Our renewal is in June, Maria. I'll be very blunt with you — if I don't have something substantially better to bring to my CISO before that renewal conversation, I don't know that we're going to be renewing.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: I hear you. And I'm not going to pretend June isn't close. It's close. That's why I want to move fast on the engineering call, on the SLA review, and on getting you that written incident report.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: What does the incident report look like? Is this going to be a two-paragraph email or are we talking something I can actually hand to a regulator if I need to?

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: It'll be a full post-mortem document — timeline, root cause analysis, impact scope, remediation steps taken, and forward-looking prevention measures. Something you can actually use.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Okay. That's... that's what I need. And I need it by end of week, not end of next week.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: End of week, I'll make it happen. I'll personally follow up with engineering today to make sure that's on track.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: I'm also going to need to understand — and this is something my CISO is going to want to know — what is your monitoring capability for the platform itself? Like, how did it take six hours for this to be resolved? Were you not watching your own system?

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That is a fair and frankly really important question, and I want to make sure I give you an accurate answer rather than guessing. I'll get that specifically addressed in the post-mortem, the internal detection and response timeline.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Because six hours is a long time. A long time. Something should have been paging someone well before that.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: You're absolutely right, and I share that frustration honestly. Okay — so let me recap what I'm committing to so we're both clear. Written confirmation of event data integrity today, full post-mortem document by end of this week, executive and engineering call with you and your CISO early next week, and a review of your SLA tier. Does that cover the immediate priorities?

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Those are the immediate priorities, yes. But Maria — I want you to understand where I'm sitting. This isn't just about the outage anymore. It's about whether Aegis Detect is the right platform for us long term. And I'm going to be doing my own due diligence on that regardless of what you put in front of me.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: I would expect nothing less from someone in your role. And I'm not afraid of that evaluation — I think when you look at total capability, especially when you factor in how Detect integrates with Comply and Identity for your HIPAA obligations, that story is still very strong. But I have to earn that back. I know that.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Yeah. You do. Alright. I need to get back to my team, they're still dealing with the backfill validation and I want to make sure we're not missing anything. Send me that data integrity confirmation as soon as you can.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: You'll have it within the hour. Grace, thank you for staying on the call with me. I know this wasn't easy and I'm going to do everything I can to make this right.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: I appreciate that. I really do. Just... don't let me down on the follow-through, okay? That's the part that's going to matter.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: I won't. Talk soon, Grace.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Okay. Bye.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Okay, I think we've got everyone. Tom, you there? Brian's on, Ananya's on — Sofia, can you confirm?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Yeah I'm here, sorry, I was just pulling up the dashboards.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Good. Okay, let's not waste time. Tom, do you want to kick us off or should I?

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: I'll start. So, uh, at approximately 9:14 AM Pacific we started seeing alerts — or rather, the absence of alerts — from the Detect event processing pipeline. The ingestion layer just... stopped. No events coming through, no throughput, and the monitoring didn't catch it immediately which is its own problem we're going to talk about.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: When you say the monitoring didn't catch it — are we talking about our internal alerting or the customer-facing status stuff?

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Both, honestly. Internal alerting had a lag, and the status page wasn't updated until... Ananya, when did we actually post to the status page?

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: It was around 10:40, so that's — yeah, that's about an hour and a half after the initial failure. And I want to be upfront, that's on me partly because I was the one who was supposed to pull the trigger on that and I was heads-down trying to figure out the root cause first.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Ananya, I appreciate you saying that but that's a process failure, not a you failure. We shouldn't have a single person making the call on when to post. That's a systemic issue.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: I mean — I agree with that, but we can talk process later. Right now I've got customers blowing up my team's queue and I need to understand what actually happened technically so I can give my support engineers something to say.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Right, yeah. Sofia, you want to walk through the technical failure? You were in the weeds on this from the start.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Sure. So the event ingestion pipeline — basically it's a single primary node that handles all incoming threat event data before it fans out to the processing workers. That node hit a memory ceiling, started throwing OOM errors, and because we don't have a circuit breaker or any redundancy on that node, it just cascaded. Everything downstream starved out.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Wait, so there's literally one node doing all of the ingestion? That's... okay I'm not an engineer but that sounds like a really bad design.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: It's not ideal, no. It's — look, it's a legacy architecture decision and it's been on the roadmap to address. We just hadn't prioritized it.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: It was flagged in the Q4 tech debt review, Tom. I flagged it.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: I know you did. And I'm not — look, this is not the time for that conversation. Yes, it was flagged, yes, it should have been prioritized, and now we're dealing with the consequences. I own that.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Okay. So what's the current status? Are customers seeing data right now or are we still down?

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: We're still down as of this call. Sofia and I have been working on a hotfix — we're spinning up a secondary ingestion node and routing traffic through a temporary load balancer config, but it's not clean. It's a stopgap.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: How long until that stopgap is live?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Best case? Another hour, maybe ninety minutes. But I don't want to commit to that publicly yet.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Brian, what are you hearing from customers? Like, who's screaming the loudest right now?

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: I mean, everyone in the enterprise tier is screaming. We've got — let me just look at my notes here — Harmon Financial has escalated twice, they're threatening to call their account manager directly. TerraLogix is asking whether this is a breach, which is a great question to get at 10 in the morning. And we've got at least a dozen mid-market customers who are just, you know, sitting there with a broken security product and no visibility into their threat environment.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: TerraLogix thinks it's a breach? We need to get ahead of that immediately.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: I know, I've got one of my senior support reps on the phone with them right now. But Tom, I need an actual statement I can give customers about what happened. Something honest that doesn't make us look like we don't know what we're doing.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: The problem is we're still root-causing. I don't want to put out a statement that's wrong.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Tom, we know enough. It's a pipeline failure. It's not a breach. That's the most important thing to communicate right now. Customers would rather have an incomplete honest update than silence.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: I agree with Brian on this one. We've been in communications blackout for too long already.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Fine. Fine. I'll draft something in the next — can someone give me fifteen minutes and I'll have a customer statement?

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Please. And make sure it explicitly says this is not a security breach, that's the first question everyone's asking.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Yeah, I will. Sofia, while I'm doing that — can you document what you know so far about the failure sequence? We're going to need a timeline for the post-mortem.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Already started. I've got a doc in Confluence, I'll drop the link in Slack. One thing I want to flag though — when we bring the stopgap online, customers are going to start seeing alerts again but there may be a gap in their event history. Like, events that occurred during the outage window may not be recoverable. We need to be upfront about that.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Oh, that's — yeah, that's going to be a very bad conversation to have with customers in regulated industries. Are we sure about that? Is there any way to replay the events?

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: We have some buffering at the source layer but it's limited. I'd say we can recover maybe sixty to seventy percent of what was lost. The rest is... gone.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Okay. That's — we need to think carefully about how we communicate that. That's a significant data loss event for a security monitoring product.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Agreed. Let's not bury it. Put it in the post-incident report. Customers deserve to know.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Can we talk about the immediate next steps? I just want to make sure we have a clear owner on each thing before we get off this call, because the last two hours have been a lot of people doing a lot of things without coordination.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Yes. Okay. Sofia and Ananya, you own the stopgap fix and the technical timeline doc. Brian, you own customer communications and escalation triage. I'll draft the public statement and then I'm going to get on a call with the CTO because she's going to need to know about this if she doesn't already.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: She knows. I got a Slack from her about forty minutes ago.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Great. That's... great. Okay. Let's reconvene in ninety minutes or sooner if the fix goes live. Everybody check in on Slack. And — I know this has been a rough morning. Let's just get it fixed.

speaker: Brian Cho
sentence: Agreed. Let's go.

sentence: Uh... actually, yeah, there was one more thing. The page numbers in the footer — they seem off. Like page one of twelve or something, but the document is clearly more than twelve pages.

sentence: Yep, likely all the same root cause in the PDF renderer. speaker: Warren Beck
sentence: Good morning everyone, thanks for jumping on — Lisa, Maria, can you hear us okay?

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Yeah, loud and clear Warren, good morning! Thanks for making time for this — I know your calendar's been pretty packed lately.

sentence: Morning! Really excited for this one, we have a lot of good stuff to walk through today.

sentence: Morning everyone, Julia Tran here from the security team — looking forward to digging into the HIPAA piece specifically.

sentence: Perfect, yeah, that's really the main focus for us today. Um, so just to set the stage a little — Warren, do you want to give everyone a quick overview of where Blackridge is at from a compliance standpoint before we jump in?

sentence: Sure, yeah, happy to. speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Yeah, that's such a common story and it's — honestly it's one of the exact things that the new Comply v2 release was built to address. Julia, I'm curious, from your perspective on the analyst side, where were the biggest friction points in that audit process?

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: So, um, the biggest thing for me was just the evidence gathering. Like I was spending, I don't know, probably two full weeks before the audit just pulling logs from different systems, trying to reconcile timestamps, making sure our access control documentation was current. It was... yeah, it was a lot of manual effort that felt like it shouldn't have to be manual.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Two weeks — yeah, that's significant. And that's two weeks of your time that's not going toward actual security work.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Exactly, exactly. And we had a near-miss on a deadline last time because of it, so Warren kind of lit a fire under us to find a better solution.

speaker: Warren Beck
sentence: Right, and that's — that's kind of what led us back to this conversation with Aegis. We've been running Protect and Identity for about, what, eight months now? speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Yeah, exactly, and honestly Warren your timing could not be better. speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: When you say on-demand, are we talking like, I click a button and it's done in minutes, or is there still some configuration and setup time involved?

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Great question. So there's an initial configuration pass — you're mapping your environment, defining your scope, connecting your data sources — and that typically takes, uh, Maria correct me if I'm off, but somewhere in the range of a few days to maybe a week depending on environment complexity?

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Yeah, that's right. For a environment like yours where you already have Aegis Identity and Protect running, honestly it could be even faster because we're already pulling a lot of the relevant signals. The IAM data, the access logs, the backup records — a lot of that is already flowing into the platform. So once Comply is scoped and configured, generating a HIPAA report is... yeah, it's essentially a few clicks.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Okay, that's — that's actually really compelling. Because right now it's like, we have all this data in various places and it's just not... connected in any meaningful way for audit purposes.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That's exactly the problem Comply v2 was designed to solve. And you mentioned HIPAA but I do want to flag — the multi-framework support is a big part of this release too. So you get SOC 2, PCI DSS, ISO 27001 all in the same platform. Given that you're in financial services, I'd imagine PCI DSS is also on your radar.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Oh yeah, absolutely. We have a separate compliance cycle for PCI and right now that's managed by a completely different team using completely different tools, so the idea of consolidating that is... yeah, Warren, that's something we've talked about.

speaker: Warren Beck
sentence: We have, yeah. I mean, the consolidation story is attractive from a cost standpoint and also just from an operational standpoint. Having one pane of glass for compliance across frameworks — that's something our board has actually been asking about.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: That's great to hear, and I think that's a really strong use case we can build out for you. But let's stay focused on HIPAA for now since that's the urgent one — we can absolutely come back to the multi-framework story. Maria, do you want to walk through what a HIPAA report actually looks like in v2?

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Yeah, definitely. So I'm going to pull up a sample report here — bear with me one second — okay, so what you're seeing is the executive summary view, which is what you'd typically hand to an auditor or your leadership team. It maps your controls directly to the HIPAA Security Rule safeguards — administrative, physical, technical. Each control has a status, an evidence log, and a timestamp. Auditors love the timestamp piece because it removes a lot of the back and forth.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Oh, that's really clean actually. Can you click into — um, can you show the technical safeguards section? That's typically where we have the most scrutiny.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Of course, yeah. So here you can see access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, transmission security — each one has a detailed evidence panel. And if you have a gap, it'll flag it and give you a recommended remediation action. It's not just telling you that you have a problem, it's pointing you toward how to fix it.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Okay I have to say, that remediation guidance piece is something we're missing completely right now. Like our current process is finding a gap and then going okay, now what? And it usually involves a lot of internal back and forth.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Yeah, and that back and forth is where time really bleeds out. And honestly, Warren, from a CISO perspective, that remediation guidance also gives you a cleaner audit trail to show that gaps were identified and actioned — which is exactly what regulators want to see.

speaker: Warren Beck
sentence: That's a really good point. The ability to demonstrate that we have a process for finding and remediating issues is almost as important as the issues themselves in the eyes of HHS.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Exactly, yeah. So, um, I do want to just mention one thing — and Maria, jump in here — but we did have a period of instability on the Detect side back in March, some of you may have been aware of that. We were very upfront about it. That was a six-hour window where event processing had an issue. That's been fully resolved with redundant nodes and a circuit breaker pattern, and I want to be transparent that we took that very seriously.

speaker: Warren Beck
sentence: Yeah, we did see the incident notification. Appreciate that you were proactive about communicating it. Honestly, the transparency there actually... it increased my confidence in Aegis a little, weirdly enough. Like you didn't try to hide it.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That means a lot to hear, Warren, really. And just to be clear, Comply v2 — the reporting infrastructure — runs on a completely separate pipeline from the Detect event processing, so that incident has no bearing on the compliance reporting capabilities.

speaker: Warren Beck
sentence: Good to know, and yeah, I didn't think it was related but it's good to have that confirmed. Julia, any other questions on the technical side before we talk about next steps?

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Um, yeah, one thing — what does the scheduling look like? Can we set up automated recurring reports or is it purely on-demand?

sentence: Both, actually. speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Oh that's a nice feature. Yeah, I currently send Warren a monthly compliance status email that takes me like an hour to put together. That's... I could see that going away.

speaker: Warren Beck
sentence: Ha, one less thing on your plate, Julia.

sentence: I'll take it! Um, one more question — and this is maybe a bigger one — what does the integration look like with our existing SIEM? We're running LogVault.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: LogVault is fully supported, yeah. We have a native connector — it's not a custom integration, it's out of the box. So Comply can pull relevant log data from LogVault as part of the evidence collection, which again reduces the manual work significantly.

speaker: Julia Tran
sentence: Okay, that's a big one for us. We were a little worried we'd have to do some heavy lifting on the integration side but that's — that's reassuring.

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: So from a next steps standpoint — and I want to make sure we're leaving this call with something concrete — I'd love to propose that we put together a scoped proof of concept specifically for the HIPAA reporting against your Blackridge Health Finance environment. sentence: Yeah, I think that makes a lot of sense. Warren, what do you think?

speaker: Lisa Park
sentence: Absolutely, I'll have something over to you by end of week. And I'll include a contact from our implementation team so Julia can connect directly with them on the technical environment questions. Maria, anything else you want to cover before we close out?

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Hi, this is Priya from Aegis Cloud Security support, I have Sarah on the line with me as well — she's one of our senior engineers on the Protect side. Am I speaking with Yuki?

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Yeah, hi, Yuki Tanaka here, Security Lead at Pineridge. Thanks for getting back to me, I know I submitted the case like yesterday afternoon so I appreciate the quick turnaround.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Of course, yeah. So we've pulled up Case 2638 — the CloudPrime S3 backup connector issue. Before Sarah digs in, do you want to just give us a quick rundown of what you're seeing on your end?

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Sure, yeah. So basically, we set up the S3 connector in Aegis Protect about three weeks ago, it was working fine initially. But starting around, I want to say, last Thursday or Friday, we started seeing backup jobs failing partway through. They initiate, they run for a bit, and then just... stop. No completion, no error that makes a ton of sense.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Okay, and when you say no error that makes sense — what is the error message actually showing? Like is it a timeout, a permissions thing, or something else?

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: It says — let me actually pull it up — it says, uh, 'connector write operation terminated, upstream response code 403.' But here's the thing, the IAM permissions haven't changed. Like, nothing on our CloudPrime side changed that week.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Got it, a 403. Yeah that's a good starting point. Sarah, do you want to walk through what that typically maps to in the connector?

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Yeah, so a 403 from the S3 connector can actually come from a few different places, it's not always what it looks like on the surface. The first thing I'd want to rule out is whether the IAM role that Aegis Protect is assuming has the right combination of permissions — specifically s3:PutObject, s3:GetBucketLocation, and then also the bucket policy itself. Sometimes people set up the IAM role correctly but the bucket policy has a deny statement that overrides it.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Okay, so the bucket policy can override the IAM role permissions — yeah, that's a good call, I can check that. We were pretty careful when we set this up but it's possible something got changed on the bucket side.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Exactly, yeah. And the other thing — do you happen to know if the S3 bucket has any VPC endpoint policies or SCPs at the organization level? Like CloudPrime Service Control Policies? Those can also silently block writes even when IAM looks clean.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Hmm. SCPs... we do have some org-level policies but I'd have to check with our CloudPrime admin to see if anything changed there. We had a routine policy review around that time actually, now that I think about it.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: That's actually really useful context, yeah. That timing aligns with when you started seeing the failures, so it's worth checking whether any of those policy changes could have inadvertently restricted the role Aegis is using.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Yeah, I can loop in our CloudPrime admin after this call. Is there a specific role name or ARN I should reference? I want to make sure they're looking at the right thing.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Yes, so in your Aegis Protect console, if you go to Integrations and then Connector Settings for the S3 connector, you'll see the IAM role ARN that Aegis is using listed under Authentication Details. That's the one your admin should be checking.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Okay, let me — hold on, I actually have the console open. Integrations... Connector Settings... yeah, okay I see it. The ARN is there, I can copy that out.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Perfect. And while you're in there, can you also check what region the connector is configured to target? The reason I ask is — we've seen a specific edge case where if the bucket is in one region but the connector config still has the default us-east-1 endpoint, it can cause issues that surface as 403s rather than the more obvious region mismatch error.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Oh interesting. Uh, the bucket is in us-west-2. And the connector says... endpoint is set to s3.amazonaws.com, no region specified explicitly.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Yeah, so that could actually be a contributing factor. The generic s3.amazonaws.com endpoint will route to us-east-1 by default, and depending on your bucket's config — especially if you have bucket policies that restrict access to specific regions or VPCs — that cross-region request can get blocked.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: So I should change that endpoint to the region-specific one? Like s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com?

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Exactly, yeah, that would be the recommended change. You'd want to update that in the connector settings and then re-test a backup job. It's a pretty quick change to make.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Okay, that makes sense. And should I do that before or after I check the SCP stuff with the CloudPrime admin? Like what order would you tackle these?

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: I'd actually do the endpoint fix first since you can do that right now without needing anyone else, and then run a test job. If it still fails, then you have a cleaner picture to bring to your CloudPrime admin — you can rule out the endpoint issue and focus the conversation on the policy side.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: That's a good approach, yeah. Okay, I'm going to make that endpoint change now actually, while we're on the call. Bear with me a second.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Take your time.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Alright, updated. Saved. So I'll kick off a test backup — it's going to take a few minutes to run. I might not know the result before we hang up but at least I know what to look for.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: That's totally fine. We'll keep the case open and you can reply directly to the case email with the results. If it still 403s after the endpoint fix, Sarah can pull the connector logs from our side and do a deeper trace.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Yeah, and if you want to share the CloudTrail logs from your CloudPrime account for that time window, that would help a lot too — it'll show exactly what API call was being made and why it was denied.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Yep, I can pull CloudTrail logs. We have a 30-day retention window so that's no problem. I'll grab the logs from last Thursday through today and attach them to the case.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Perfect, that would be really helpful. Okay, so to summarize where we are — you're testing the endpoint fix first, and then pending that result, you'll check with your CloudPrime admin about the SCP policy review and share CloudTrail logs with us. Does that all sound right?

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Yeah, that's the plan. One other thing — is there any way to configure Aegis Protect to retry backup jobs automatically if they fail? Right now it seems like it just stops and I have to manually re-trigger it.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Yes, there is actually. In the connector settings, under Advanced Options, there's a Retry Policy section — you can configure the number of retry attempts and the backoff interval. It's not enabled by default so it might just be turned off in your config.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Oh, I didn't even know that was there. I'll look for that when I'm in the settings. That'll help in the meantime even if the root cause isn't fully resolved yet.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Yeah, definitely. It won't fix the underlying issue but it should reduce the manual overhead while we sort this out.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Alright, I think I have enough to work with. I appreciate you both jumping on this — Sarah, the region endpoint thing especially, I wouldn't have caught that on my own.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Happy to help. It's one of those things that trips people up because the error message doesn't point directly at it. Hopefully that's the main culprit.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: We'll keep an eye on the case and respond as soon as you send over the test results and the CloudTrail logs. Shouldn't be more than a business day turnaround from us.

speaker: Yuki Tanaka
sentence: Works for me. Thanks again, talk soon.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Thanks Yuki, take care.
