sentence: Okay so — this is the post-incident review for the Detect outage, March 10th through the 18th, well, the outage itself was March 10th, we're just closing it out today.

sentence: Sounds good.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: So the incident started around 2:14 AM Pacific on March 10th. The event ingestion pipeline — this is the core processing layer that pulls in telemetry from customer environments — it had a cascading failure. Basically one node went down, and because we had a single point of failure in that ingestion layer, the whole pipeline backed up and eventually stopped processing.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: And that's what caused the six-hour window with no visibility?

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Correct. For approximately six hours, customers were not getting threat monitoring alerts. Events were being generated on their end, but we weren't processing them, so nothing was surfacing in the Detect dashboard.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Six hours is — I mean, that's a long time for a security product. Some of my customers were pretty upset, understandably.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: I know, and I'm not going to sugarcoat it — this was a significant failure. The good news is we've done a full root cause analysis and the fix is already deployed.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Can you explain the fix at a level I can actually relay to customers? Like, not super deep in the weeds but enough that it's credible.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Yeah, so there are two main things. First, we've added redundant processing nodes to the event ingestion layer. So if one node fails, traffic automatically routes to backup nodes — no more single point of failure. Second, we've implemented what's called a circuit breaker pattern, which basically means if one part of the pipeline starts to degrade, it isolates that failure instead of letting it cascade through the whole system.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Okay, that's actually pretty explainable. Redundancy and failure isolation — I can work with that.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: And just to add to that — from a product standpoint, we're also looking at adding a visible status indicator in the Detect UI so customers can see in real-time whether the pipeline is healthy. That's not shipped yet but it's on the near-term roadmap.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: When's that realistically landing?

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Uh, we're targeting end of April, but I don't want to over-promise. It depends on a few other things in the queue.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Can we at least tell customers it's in progress? Because right now they're asking 'how do I even know if Detect is working' and I don't have a great answer.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Yeah, absolutely tell them it's in progress. That's a real commitment, end of April is the target.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: I do want to flag one thing — and this is a bit of a sore spot for me — our alerting on the pipeline health itself was inadequate. We didn't catch this until customers started reaching out. That's not acceptable.

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Yeah, how did we find out? Was it a customer ticket?

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: It was — one of Diana's team's accounts, actually. Flagged that they hadn't seen any alerts in a few hours and opened a support ticket.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Yeah, that was Thornfield Financial. They've got a pretty attentive security team, fortunately.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: So we are adding internal monitoring on the ingestion pipeline itself — not just the output but the pipeline health metrics. That's already in place as of this week.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Good. I think one of the things I keep hearing from customers is — they feel like they're flying blind sometimes with Detect. And this incident kind of reinforced that. I don't say that to be harsh, I just think it's worth naming.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: No, I — yeah. I hear that. And honestly some of it is a product maturity issue, some of it is infrastructure. Megan, is there anything we can prioritize in the short term to help with that visibility gap beyond the status indicator?

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: We've been talking about a heartbeat alert feature — basically Detect would proactively notify customers if it stops receiving telemetry from their environment for more than, say, fifteen minutes. That would at least give them a signal on their end.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Oh, that would be huge actually. Can we pull that forward?

speaker: Megan Lawson
sentence: Let me take a look at the roadmap this week. I think there's room to move it up, but I need to see what it bumps. I'll come back to both of you by end of week.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Okay. And on the customer communication side — Diana, what's the current state? Did we send a post-incident report to affected accounts?

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: We sent an initial communication on March 10th when the incident was resolved, but we have not sent a formal PIR document yet. I've been waiting on the root cause writeup from engineering.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Yeah that's on me, I'll have that to you by tomorrow morning. Full technical summary, the fix, and what we're doing to prevent recurrence.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Okay, and then I'll adapt it into customer-facing language and get it out by end of week. Some of our enterprise accounts are going to want a call, not just an email — particularly the ones who are mid-renewal.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Probably Tom for anyone who wants a technical deep-dive. I'll flag the specific accounts and we can coordinate. I don't think it'll be more than three or four.

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Yeah, I'm available for that. Just loop me in with at least a day's notice if you can.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Will do. Alright, I think I have what I need. Tom — PIR doc tomorrow, and Megan — roadmap check by end of week on the heartbeat feature?

speaker: Tom Bradley
sentence: Correct.

speaker: Megan Lawson

sentence: Alright, good. I want that prioritized. Now, let's go back to the Detect post-mortem follow-ups because I know we've been chipping away at those for a month now and I want to make sure we're not letting things slip. Ravi, where are we on the full list?

sentence: Okay, that's the runbook. What else is outstanding?

sentence: The load testing suite. We said we were going to build out a proper synthetic load test to simulate cascading failure scenarios so we can catch this kind of thing before it hits production. That one hasn't been started.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Yeah, that's a bigger lift. I mean, it's important but it's not a one-day thing.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: I know it's not, but we committed to it in the post-mortem document and I don't want it to become one of those things that just... never happens. Can we put a realistic timeline on it?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: I mean, if we're being honest, with the Comply bug work and the circuit breaker PR, I don't think either of us has bandwidth to start that before, um, end of April at the earliest.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: End of April to start, or end of April to finish?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: To start. A full load testing suite for the event ingestion pipeline — even a basic one — I'd estimate two weeks of actual work once we get into it.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Okay. Let's say we target starting that the week of April 28th, and we plan for a two-week sprint on it. That puts us at mid-May, which I think is acceptable given the other priorities. But I want it on the roadmap formally, not just as a verbal commitment.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: I'll add it to Jira today.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Good. Now — Sofia, you mentioned wanting to talk through something about the circuit breaker PR. Let's do that before we run out of time.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Right, yeah. So the way Ravi has the failover logic set up, when the circuit breaker opens it routes traffic to the secondary node immediately. My concern is there's no health check before the reroute — so if the secondary node happens to also be degraded, we're just... blindly routing to a bad node.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: I mean, the assumption was the secondary is always healthy because it's not under load, but yeah, I can see the issue. You're saying we should run a quick health probe before committing the reroute?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Exactly. Even just a lightweight ping with a 500ms timeout — if it fails, we surface the alert and don't silently fail over to a bad node.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: That's a reasonable concern. Ravi, how much work is that to add?

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: Honestly not a lot. Maybe a few hours. I don't want to hold up the whole PR for it, could I add it as a follow-up ticket?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: I'd rather have it in the same PR, to be honest. It's a safety thing and it's directly part of the circuit breaker behavior. I don't love shipping the pattern without it.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: I agree with Sofia on this one. It's a few hours of work, let's just do it right. Update the PR and ping Sofia when it's ready.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: Yeah, fair enough. I'll have it updated by end of day.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Perfect. Okay, I think we're at about time. Any other blockers before we wrap?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Nothing from me.

speaker: Ravi Gupta
sentence: Same. I'm good.

speaker: Raj Kapoor
sentence: Alright. To summarize — Comply timeout fix is Sofia's priority, Ravi assists with QA. Circuit breaker PR gets the health check added today, then Sofia reviews and we merge. Runbook gets finished this week. And load testing goes on the roadmap for April 28th start. I'll send a recap in Slack. Good work, everyone — talk next week.

sentence: We don't. And that's a gap. Like we put out a postmortem internally and we sent customer comms but there's nothing packaged for sales conversations.

sentence: VaultEdge is mostly showing up in deals where Protect is the primary use case. sentence: That's a problem. We should not be nodding. We need either our own benchmarks or a way to reframe the conversation around total recovery workflow, not just raw RTO. Because point-in-time recovery, data integrity validation — those are things we do well and VaultEdge's pitch glosses over.

sentence: Okay that's good to hear. But let's not let that distract from the Q1 problems. The concern I have is that we go into Q2 still carrying this reliability baggage from March and we don't have good answers for it.

sentence: Yeah, absolutely, and I was going to bring that up myself. I didn't want to just skip over it. That was — that was not a good situation and I know it caused real disruption for your team.

sentence: Six hours, Daniel. Six hours where we had basically zero visibility into threat monitoring. In a manufacturing environment that's... I mean, our OT systems, our floor networks — we can't just go dark on threat detection for six hours.

sentence: Sure. We've been running that in production for about a week now and it's been stable.

speaker: Harold Jensen
sentence: Okay. And what's the — I guess, what's your confidence level that this doesn't happen again? Because that's really what I need to be able to tell my leadership.

sentence: I'd say we're significantly more resilient now than we were before. That specific failure mode is addressed. I won't tell you it'll never have any downtime ever, but a repeat of that exact scenario — the cascading failure — that's been architecturally corrected.

sentence: We're also — and this is something I can put in writing as part of the renewal package — we're offering an enhanced SLA on Detect uptime going forward. I want to make sure that's formalized, not just a verbal commitment.

sentence: That's good. I mean, that's the kind of thing I need to see on paper. What does that SLA look like, specifically?

speaker: Daniel Okafor
sentence: We're proposing 99.9% uptime on Detect, which is up from the 99.5% that was in your current contract. And there are remediation credits tied to that if we fall below. I can send you the exact language after this call.

sentence: So you're currently on a three-module package — Protect, Detect, and Comply. The base renewal would be a like-for-like at a roughly four percent uplift given, uh, standard CPI adjustments. sentence: Yeah, Identity is not on the table right now. We're handling IAM internally and it's not a priority to change that in the near term. The four percent — look, I expected some kind of increase, but I'm going to need to justify that internally given what happened with the outage.

sentence: That's a fair point, and I'm not going to pretend the timing isn't awkward. What I'd like to do is see if there's a middle ground — maybe we can look at the structure of the deal, whether a two-year term instead of one year gives us room to soften that increase on year one.

speaker: Harold Jensen
sentence: I'd be open to hearing the numbers on that. sentence: I appreciate you being straight with us on that. Can I ask — is that conversation driven mainly by the outage, or is there something about the product itself that's prompting it?

sentence: Yeah, that's a good segue actually. So — Sofia, you want to touch on where Comply is heading? Because there's some relevant context there.

sentence: That's a reasonable position. I guess I'd want to see that in writing too. I've been burned before by roadmap promises that just... didn't land.

sentence: Yeah, that's the bulk of it. And I guess just... look, the relationship matters. We've been on the platform for two and a half years. I'm not trying to blow that up over one bad month. But I do need the business case to be solid.

sentence: I hear you and I think that's completely reasonable. sentence: Understood, completely understood. Can we also loop back to the Detect overage real quick before I forget — Marcus, the eight hundred and forty dollars in event ingestion overage, what's driving that?

sentence: So for Detect, looking at the event volume data — there was a spike in ingestion on March 10th through March 16th, which is... okay that's actually during the outage window. That's interesting.

sentence: During the outage? How are we being charged for events during a period when your system wasn't even processing them properly? We had six hours of no visibility. We were flying blind.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Yeah, that — I need to look more closely at this because if events were queued and then replayed through the pipeline after the fix was applied, it's possible the billing system counted them against the current cycle even though a chunk of that volume was essentially backfill. sentence: So we might be getting charged for the retry traffic that your system generated because of your own outage. Is that what you're telling me?

sentence: I don't want to confirm that definitively until Marcus can verify the timestamps, but it is a plausible explanation and if that's what happened, that charge should absolutely not stand.

sentence: Marcus, can you get into the event ingestion logs for that period and flag the volume breakdown — specifically anything that looks like queued replay events from after March 16th?

sentence: Yeah, I'll need to pull that from the backend pipeline logs, that's going to take me a bit longer than this call, but I can have something documented by end of day.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Hi, this is David Kim calling from Aegis Cloud Security support, I have case number 3536 here for Stratos Cloud — am I speaking with Damien?

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Yeah, yeah that's me. Damien Rowe. Thanks for calling back, I've been waiting on this for like — look, we submitted this ticket yesterday morning and this is kind of urgent for us.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I completely understand, Damien, and I apologize for the wait. I also have my team lead Priya Patel joining us on the call today, she's been looped in given the priority of the case.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Hi Damien, thanks for your patience. We definitely want to get to the bottom of this with you today.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Okay, sure. I appreciate that. So — look, I'll just get into it. We've been running Aegis Detect integrated with our LogVault environment, right, and starting around — I want to say April 18th, maybe 19th — we started seeing consistent timeout errors when Detect tries to push event data to our LogVault HTTP Event Collector endpoint. It's not intermittent, it's basically every... every batch that goes through.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Okay, so consistent timeouts hitting the HEC endpoint. And when you say every batch — are these on a scheduled interval or is this continuous streaming from Detect?

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: We have it set to push every five minutes. And it was working fine, totally fine, for months before this. Nothing changed on our end — we didn't update LogVault, we didn't change the HEC token, nothing. So whatever broke, it broke on your side.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I hear you, and I want to be careful not to make assumptions before we dig into the logs, but I do want to look at this thoroughly. Can I ask — are you seeing any error codes coming back, or is it just a straight connection timeout with no response from the HEC endpoint?

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: It's a connection timeout. We're not even getting a response code. The connection just... hangs and then drops. Our LogVault team confirmed the HEC endpoint itself is healthy — they're receiving data from other sources without any issues.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Okay so LogVault HEC is healthy, other sources pushing fine, only Aegis Detect is timing out. David, I'm wondering if this could be related to the event processing changes that were pushed in the pipeline update earlier this month.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Yeah, I was thinking the same thing. Damien, I want to ask — do you happen to know your Aegis Detect tenant version? It should be visible in the platform under Settings and then About.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Uh, hold on. Let me — yeah, give me a second, I'm pulling it up now. Okay it says... Detect pipeline version 4.1.2, event processor build April 8th.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: April 8th build. Okay. Priya, that would be the post-remediation build from after the March incident, right?

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Yes, that's correct. So Damien, I don't know if you were made aware — in mid-March we had a significant incident with the Aegis Detect event processing pipeline. There was a cascading failure that caused monitoring gaps, and we pushed a remediation update that included redundant processing nodes and a circuit breaker pattern in the ingestion layer.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Oh, I am very aware of that outage. Yeah, we were one of the customers affected. Six hours of no visibility — that was a really bad week for us. We had to explain to our own leadership why we had blind spots in our threat monitoring. That was not a fun conversation.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Yeah, we — we really do understand that, and I'm sorry again for the disruption that caused. What I'm wondering now though is whether the circuit breaker implementation in that fix might be contributing to what you're seeing with LogVault. If the outbound connector to your HEC endpoint is getting tripped by the circuit breaker under certain load conditions, that could explain the timeout pattern.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: So you're telling me the fix for one problem might have caused a new problem? That's — I mean, come on. That's not great to hear.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: I want to be clear that I'm not confirming that yet, we need to look at the logs before I say anything definitively. But it's a hypothesis worth investigating. David, can you pull the outbound connector logs for Stratos on the Detect side?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Already on it. Damien, while I'm doing that — can you tell me roughly what volume of events Detect is processing in your environment? Like ballpark events per minute during peak hours?

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Uh, we're a pretty active environment. I'd say during peak — probably somewhere between eight and twelve thousand events per minute. We're a technology company, we've got a lot of infrastructure generating signal.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Okay, yeah that's — that's actually useful context. So I'm looking at the connector logs now and... okay, yeah. I'm seeing the circuit breaker state flipping to open on the LogVault HEC connector. It looks like it's tripping because the payload size on the five-minute batch is exceeding the default threshold that was set in the 4.1.2 build.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: So what does that mean exactly? In plain terms.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: So the circuit breaker that was added to protect the pipeline — it has a payload threshold, and at your event volume, the five-minute batch is generating a payload that's hitting that limit. When the circuit breaker trips, it stops the outbound push to LogVault and the connection just drops, which is why you're seeing the timeout with no response code.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: So it's stopping itself from sending data to our SIEM. That means we've had gaps in our LogVault data since the 18th? That's — that's four days of incomplete data. Do you understand what that means for us from a security monitoring standpoint?

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: I do, and I want to be transparent with you — yes, the events during the tripped periods would not have been forwarded to LogVault. They are retained within Aegis Detect itself, so the data isn't lost, but it hasn't been pushed to your HEC endpoint during those windows.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Okay so the data exists on your side. Can we backfill it? Can you push the missed events retroactively to our LogVault instance?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: That's actually something we can do, yes. We have a manual replay function for the outbound connector. What I'd want to do first though is fix the underlying threshold issue so we're not in the same situation after the backfill.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Yes, absolutely do that first. What's the fix?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: So we have two options. One — we can adjust the circuit breaker payload threshold for your tenant specifically to accommodate your event volume. Two — we can switch your LogVault integration to use a streaming mode instead of batched pushes, which would distribute the load more evenly and avoid hitting the threshold altogether. Honestly for your volume, streaming is probably the better long-term configuration.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: How long does switching to streaming take to configure? And is there any disruption during the switchover?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: The configuration change itself is pretty quick on our end, maybe fifteen minutes to apply and validate. There'd be a brief pause in forwarding during the switch — we're talking under a minute typically. And then we'd do the backfill right after to make sure you have everything.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Okay. Do the streaming mode. And I want a written summary of what happened here — the root cause, the timeline, what data was affected, and what was done to fix it. I need to take that to my leadership and frankly I'm going to need to share it with our security team so they can assess any gaps in coverage.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Absolutely, Damien. I'll personally make sure a detailed incident summary is drafted and sent to you — I'd say end of business tomorrow at the latest. And I want to flag that given this is the second significant disruption to your Detect environment this year, I'd like to schedule a call with you and our customer success team to review your overall configuration and make sure we're set up in a way that's more resilient for your environment going forward.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Yeah, that conversation needs to happen. I'll be honest with you — my team has been asking questions about whether Aegis Detect is the right platform for us. We're a fast-growing company, we can't be absorbing incidents like this. So that conversation with customer success, fine, but it better be substantive.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Understood, and I appreciate you being direct with us about that. That feedback is important and I won't minimize it. Let's get the immediate fix applied today, get you the backfill, and then we'll make sure the follow-up conversation is meaningful. David, can you go ahead and initiate the streaming mode switch for Stratos now?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Yeah, doing it now. Damien, you might see a very brief pause in the LogVault feed — like I said, under a minute — and then it should resume and you'll see the stream mode active. I'll confirm with you on the call when it's validated.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Fine. And the backfill — how long will that take to actually show up in LogVault?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Given the volume we're talking about, probably one to two hours for the full four-day backfill to complete. It runs as a background job. I'll send you a confirmation email when it kicks off and another when it completes.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Alright. I'll take that. Look — I appreciate you both getting on the call and actually diagnosing this today. I'm still frustrated with the situation, I won't pretend I'm not. But at least we have a path forward.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: That's completely fair, Damien. We'll get everything wrapped up and make sure you have the documentation you need. Keep an eye out for my email and I'll be in touch as soon as the backfill is confirmed running.

speaker: Damien Rowe
sentence: Sounds good. Thanks.

sentence: That's — yeah, I hear you and that's not okay. Inconsistent MFA enforcement in financial services, I mean, that's not just a technical inconvenience, that's a real risk exposure for you.

sentence: Ten days with a five-day-old update on something like that is not acceptable. I'm going to personally escalate that ticket today. I'll find out exactly where it sits and who owns it and I'll get back to you by end of day with an actual status.

sentence: I appreciate that. I really do. But Maria, this isn't the first time we've had to escalate something that should have been handled at the support level, and that pattern is part of the problem.

sentence: You're right. And I'm not going to sit here and make excuses for that, because you're right. That pattern — that's something I need to take back internally and make sure we're fixing how we're handling Summit Trust's tickets. You're a strategic account and you should be treated that way.

speaker: Alicia Monroe
sentence: And I want to be fair — it's not all bad. The Comply module has been... actually things have been okay there, better than expected, and I heard there's a new version coming? We got some communication about that.

sentence: Yes! Yeah, Comply v2 just — well, it's actually going GA on April 7th, so pretty soon. On-demand reporting for SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001, all in one place. Given your compliance requirements I think that's going to be a pretty big deal for your team.

sentence: Yeah, that actually does sound useful, especially around PCI DSS. That one's always a scramble for us. But I don't want to get distracted by that because the Identity stuff is really where my head is right now.

sentence: No, totally fair. Let's stay on Identity. So MFA enforcement is one thing — you said there are a few layers. What else?

sentence: The SSO session management. We're getting reports from users — and this has been happening more frequently in the last few weeks — where sessions are timing out mid-workflow. sentence: Have you — is the timeout behavior consistent with what was configured in your session policy, or is it happening earlier than expected?

sentence: Okay, that sounds like it could be a session token refresh issue — um, there's been a known behavior in certain configurations where... actually, I don't want to speculate because I might get this wrong. sentence: I'm open to it, but I need to be candid with you, Maria. We've done the 'let's schedule a technical call' thing before and sometimes those calls don't produce outcomes. So I'm willing to do it but I need there to be accountability on your side that something actually changes.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That's completely fair and I hear you. How about this — I'll commit to sending you a written summary after that call with specific action items, owners, and timelines. Not just meeting notes, like actual accountable deliverables. Would that help?

speaker: Alicia Monroe
sentence: That would help, yes. And honestly... look, I might as well be transparent with you about something. We've been having some conversations with VaultEdge. sentence: I appreciate you telling me that directly. I really do. And I'm not going to panic and start telling you VaultEdge is terrible — that's not helpful. sentence: Yeah. And I think that's fair feedback, and it's feedback I need to take seriously. Can I ask — and you don't have to answer this — is the VaultEdge conversation at a stage where it's like an active evaluation with demos and pricing, or is it more exploratory at this point?

speaker: Alicia Monroe
sentence: It's... honestly somewhere in between. We've had an intro call with them and we're supposed to see a demo next week. But I want to be clear — I'm not trying to use this as leverage. sentence: And I genuinely appreciate that. Alicia, what would it take — like if you could paint the picture for me — what would it look like for Aegis Identity to be working in a way where this conversation isn't happening? What does good look like for you?

sentence: They're not crazy asks at all. They're baseline things that should be working and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. sentence: Okay. And I want to — I do want to mention one more thing, and I know this is a separate topic but it's still in the back of my mind. That outage earlier this month. The Detect one. sentence: Yeah. The March outage — I owe you a proper conversation about that. We've been proactive with some customers but I should have reached out to you directly and sooner, and I didn't, and that's on me.

sentence: What happened exactly? The communication we got was pretty vague.

sentence: So there was a cascading failure in the event processing pipeline — it was a single point of failure in event ingestion that triggered the whole thing. It's been fixed. speaker: Alicia Monroe
sentence: No, it doesn't. And I guess what concerns me is — how do I know this kind of thing isn't going to happen again? With Identity, with Detect, with something else? That's the underlying trust issue, Maria.

sentence: That's the real question and I don't want to give you a fluffy answer. sentence: Yes. Actually yes, I think that would be useful. If he can hear it from an engineer rather than from a slide deck, that carries more weight with him.

sentence: Okay. So let me summarize what I'm taking away from today so we're aligned. I'm escalating the MFA ticket today and getting you a real status by end of day. sentence: That captures it. And Maria — I want to be clear, I'm not trying to be difficult. I want this to work. We have a lot invested in this platform and switching costs are real. But I need to be able to defend this decision internally and right now that's hard.

sentence: Okay. Good. Let's do that. And Maria — thanks for not being defensive about any of this. I know it's not the easiest call to be on.

sentence: Not the easiest, no, but it's the right one to have. I'll be in touch later today. Thanks Alicia.

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: Alright, I think we've got everyone on — Derek, can you hear us okay?

speaker: Derek Owens
sentence: Yeah, loud and clear. Good to go.

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: Perfect. Okay so, welcome everyone — I'm Rachel Torres, Senior Account Manager here at Aegis, and I have Sofia Petrov with me today, she's one of our Senior Engineers on the Identity side of things. Sofia, you wanna say a quick hi?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Hey Derek, great to meet you. Really excited to be working with Crestline on this — Identity deployments in financial services are kind of my favorite thing to dig into, so, yeah, looking forward to the conversation.

speaker: Derek Owens
sentence: Likewise, honestly. I've been looking forward to this one. We've had Protect and Detect running for a while now and the team's been happy, so adding Identity feels like a natural next step.

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: That's great to hear, and yeah, that's — that's kind of the pattern we see with a lot of our financial services customers. They start with backup and threat monitoring and then once Identity is on the roadmap it sort of becomes a priority real fast, especially given the regulatory environment you're dealing with.

speaker: Derek Owens
sentence: Exactly, yeah. We had our annual audit in February and the auditors flagged some gaps around privileged access management and MFA coverage across our admin accounts specifically. So that sort of lit a fire under us to get this moving.

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: Okay, that's — that's actually super helpful context, Derek, thank you. So Sofia, that kind of shapes where we should probably start today, right?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Yeah, absolutely. So Derek, before I get into the actual deployment steps, can you give me a quick picture of your current identity landscape? Like, what are you using today for SSO or directory services, if anything?

speaker: Derek Owens
sentence: Sure, so we're running Azure AD — or I guess it's Entra ID now, still getting used to that name — for our primary directory. We've got about 340 users total, around 40 of those are in elevated or admin roles. MFA right now is a mix of, uh, Nextera Authenticator and some legacy SMS-based auth that we've been meaning to deprecate for a while but just haven't gotten around to it.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Okay, great. And the SMS-based stuff — is that mostly for a specific group of users or is it kind of scattered across different departments?

speaker: Derek Owens
sentence: Honestly it's a bit scattered. I think it's probably 20, maybe 25 users, a lot of them are in our wealth advisory group. Some of the senior advisors have been resistant to changing their auth flow, if I'm being honest.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Ha, yeah, that's — that's a very common story. We hear that a lot. The good news is Aegis Identity has some tooling around phased MFA policy rollout that makes that transition a lot smoother. You can essentially grandfather users into a migration window rather than doing a hard cutover, which tends to reduce the, uh, the pushback significantly.

speaker: Derek Owens
sentence: Oh that's actually really good to know. My manager was asking about that specifically — like whether we'd have to do a big bang cutover. I was not looking forward to that conversation with the advisory team.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: You can tell your manager that's a hard no on the big bang approach. We've got you.

speaker: Rachel Torres
sentence: I love it. Okay so Sofia, do you want to walk Derek through sort of the high level deployment phases? I think that'll help frame the rest of the conversation.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Yeah, definitely. So, at a high level we're looking at three phases for a deployment like yours. Phase one is directory sync and SSO configuration — basically getting Aegis Identity talking to your Entra ID environment, setting up the SAML or OIDC connectors, depending on what your apps support. Phase two is MFA policy configuration and enrollment, which is where we'd handle that phased migration you mentioned. And then phase three is privileged access management, which sounds like it's actually going to be really important for you given what the auditors flagged.

speaker: Derek Owens
sentence: Yeah, the PAM piece is huge for us. Can you tell me a bit more about what that looks like in practice? Like, are we talking just session recording, or is there more to it?

sentence: So it's quite a bit more than just session recording, which I think you'll find pretty compelling. speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: All of that is reasonable and I can work toward all three. The RCA I can get you this week. speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Alright, I think we've got everyone. Diana, Chris, can you both hear me okay?

sentence: Yeah, I'm good. Just pulled up the incident doc so I can follow along.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Same here. Let me get my screen share ready in case we need to walk through the architecture diagram.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Perfect. So, uh, today's agenda — this is officially the 30-day post-incident review for the Detect outage that happened March 10th through the 18th. We want to cover what happened, where we landed with the fixes, and then Diana, I know you have some customer impact stuff you want to walk through.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Yeah, exactly. And I want to make sure we have time at the end to talk about messaging, because I'm still getting questions from a few accounts and I need to know what I can and can't say.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Totally fair, we'll get there. Tyler, do you want to just do a quick recap of the root cause first for the record?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Sure, yeah. So the short version — the event processing pipeline had a single point of failure in the event ingestion layer. What happened was we had a cascading failure that started around 11 PM on the 10th and by about 5 AM on the 11th we had essentially zero threat monitoring visibility for customers on the Detect module. Six hours of complete blindness.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: And just to be clear for this review, this wasn't a security breach, right? Like the data itself was fine, it was just the monitoring layer that went down.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Correct, yes. No data loss, no unauthorized access. The pipeline that processes incoming threat events just stopped processing. So events were queuing but nothing was being analyzed or alerted on.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Right, and that distinction actually matters a lot for how some of our customers interpreted it initially. A few of them — I'll get into this later — a few of them thought their environments had been compromised because they stopped seeing alerts.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Yeah, that was... that was a comms problem as much as a technical problem, honestly.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: We can talk about that. Let me just finish the technical recap and then Diana you can take us through the customer side. So, um, root cause confirmed as the single ingestion node — no redundancy, no failover. When that node got overloaded due to a spike in event volume from one of our larger tenants, it effectively took down the whole pipeline.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: And that's fixed now? Like what did we actually ship?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: So we shipped two things. First was redundant processing nodes — we now have three ingestion nodes running in parallel with automatic failover. If one goes down, the other two absorb the load. Second thing was implementing the circuit breaker pattern, which basically means if we detect that a downstream component is failing, the system stops trying to push work to it and routes around it instead of just hammering it until everything collapses.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: When did those go live?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: The redundant nodes were deployed March 24th. Circuit breaker pattern was fully validated and in production as of, uh, March 29th I believe.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: And we've had no recurrence since then, right? Like the 30-day window has been clean?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Yeah, clean. We had one minor hiccup on March 31st where latency spiked briefly but the circuit breaker caught it and we never lost visibility. Customers didn't see anything.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Okay. Good. That's actually — I didn't know about the March 31st thing. Was that documented somewhere?

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: It's in the internal runbook notes. Probably should have looped you in on that one just as an FYI even though it was a non-event from a customer perspective.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Yeah, I'd appreciate that going forward. Even if it's just a Slack message saying hey, we had a blip, circuit breaker fired, all good. Just so I'm not getting caught flat-footed if a customer asks.

speaker: Diana Reeves

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: What about the other two?

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: One was Meridian Financial — they're a large account, been with us about two years. Their VP of IT Security was pretty unhappy. They had an internal compliance audit scheduled for March 15th and the fact that they couldn't show continuous monitoring coverage for those six hours was a problem for them. Like a real problem, not just a perception problem.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: That's... yeah, that's rough. Is that audit risk still open for them?

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: And what's their renewal situation? Do we know?

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: They're up in August. I'm watching it closely. They haven't said anything explicitly threatening but I'm not comfortable with where the relationship is right now. We need a good next few months.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Is there anything engineering can do to help with that? Like, would it help if we could offer them some kind of enhanced monitoring visibility or a preview of upcoming Detect improvements?

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Actually, yeah, that might be worth exploring. We're planning to roll out the expanded event telemetry dashboard in Q2 anyway — could we get Meridian into a beta or an early access program?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: I'd have to check with product on timeline but I don't see why not. Chris, thoughts?

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: I think it's doable. The dashboard work is about 60% done. We could have something meaningful to show by mid-May. I'd rather not promise anything before I talk to Priya about the scope but conceptually I'm supportive.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Okay, let's flag that as a follow-up action. Who owns that conversation with Priya?

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: I'll take it. I'll ping her today and loop you in on the thread, Diana.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Great. So the third executive account — that was actually a smaller one, Treswick Healthcare. They were more worried about the regulatory angle, specifically HIPAA. They use Detect as part of their security monitoring for covered systems. Same situation — the gap in monitoring coverage was a documentation headache for them.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Did we pull the Comply team into that conversation at all? Because, um, actually — today's kind of a relevant day for this. Comply v2 just went GA this morning, right?

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Ha, yeah it did. And actually that's kind of relevant because Comply v2 has the on-demand HIPAA reporting. If Treswick was running Comply they could pull an audit trail report that would show, you know, here's exactly what happened and here's the documented gap. speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Are they on Comply currently?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: I don't know off the top of my head. I'd have to check. But if they're not, this might be a moment to have that conversation with them.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Yeah, I'll look into their contract. That's actually a good angle — turning a service recovery situation into a broader platform conversation. I like that.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Okay, so to wrap up on the action items — Tyler owns the technical documentation for the 30-day review, Chris is going to talk to Priya about the Meridian early access thing, and Diana you're going to check on Treswick's Comply status. Anything else we're missing?

sentence: I want to make sure we have a finalized customer-facing summary of the incident and the resolution. Something I can point people to if they ask. Right now I'm working from my own notes and the internal doc, but I'd like something I can actually share.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Fair enough. I'll add that to the list. Alright, I think we've covered the main bases. Is there anything else before we wrap?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: One thing I want to say — and I know this review is kind of focused on the aftermath — but I do think the actual technical response once we identified the root cause was solid. The team moved fast. I don't want that to get lost in all the... you know, the justified frustration about the outage itself.

speaker: Diana Reeves
sentence: Agreed. And I'll say that to customers too. The way the fix was communicated and deployed — that part went well. It's really the initial detection and the comms in the first two hours that I think we need to be better at next time.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: We're working on an improved alerting runbook for that. Should have it done by end of April. Alright, I'll send the meeting notes out this afternoon. Thanks everyone.

sentence: Ha, I love that type. That makes the process a lot smoother honestly. Okay so — on the Detect side specifically, I want to make sure we talk about alerting configuration because that's usually where onboarding takes the most time. You can get the data flowing pretty quickly, but tuning the alerts so you're not buried in noise — that's the art of it.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Yeah, alert fatigue is a real concern for us. Our security team is small — it's two dedicated security engineers plus myself staying involved. We can't have them chasing false positives all day.

speaker: James Mitchell
sentence: Totally understandable, and that's a really common scenario especially in retail where you've got... a lot of legitimate high-volume traffic patterns that can look suspicious if you don't tune for them. Aegis Detect has baseline learning built in — it runs in observation mode for the first seven days and builds a behavioral baseline before it starts generating production alerts.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: Oh, that's actually — I didn't know that. That's a good feature. So it won't just start firing off alerts the moment it's connected?

speaker: James Mitchell
sentence: Right, exactly. You can override that and go straight to active alerting if you want, but for most customers we recommend the observation period. Gives the system time to understand your normal traffic patterns — your order processing spikes, your batch jobs, whatever recurring patterns you have — and it significantly reduces that initial false positive noise.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: That's going to make my security team much happier. Okay. What about integrations — we use PagerDuty for incident response, and our logging goes into LogVault. Does Detect play well with those?

speaker: James Mitchell
sentence: Both are supported out of the box. PagerDuty integration is pretty much plug and play — you configure your API key in the Detect settings, map your severity levels to PagerDuty escalation policies, and alerts route through automatically. LogVault is also a native integration, we can push event data and alert context into your existing LogVault indexes.

speaker: Kendra Wallace

sentence: Good. Um — okay, I do want to ask about something. I was reading some community forums, I think it was a security subreddit, and someone mentioned there had been some reliability issues with Detect recently? I don't know if that was... accurate or if it was FUD from a competitor or what.

sentence: Yeah, I appreciate you bringing that up directly. There was an incident — it was in December, I believe — an event processing issue that caused some monitoring gaps for a period of time. I'd rather be straightforward about it than have you find out through the grapevine.

speaker: Kendra Wallace
sentence: What happened exactly?

speaker: James Mitchell
sentence: The short version is there was a failure in the event ingestion pipeline — a cascading failure that resulted in a single point of failure being exposed. Monitoring visibility was interrupted. Our engineering team identified the root cause and the fix that went in was adding redundant processing nodes and implementing a circuit breaker pattern so that kind of cascading failure can't propagate the same way again.
