speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: So the root cause was a single point of failure in our event ingestion pipeline. Essentially when one node got overwhelmed it cascaded. What we've done since is deploy redundant processing nodes across the pipeline and implement a circuit breaker pattern so that if one component starts failing it doesn't bring down the whole system. We've also increased our alerting thresholds so our on-call team gets flagged much earlier.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Okay, but — and I say this with all due respect — why wasn't that architecture in place to begin with? That's not a novel engineering concept. Circuit breakers, redundancy, these are table stakes for a platform that's supposed to be enterprise-grade.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: You're right. And I don't have a satisfying answer for that. It's a gap that shouldn't have existed and it did and we're — we're accountable for that.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: I appreciate the honesty at least. Look, we've been with Aegis for — what is it, almost three years now? This isn't coming from a place of just wanting to switch for the sake of switching. But I do have to make decisions that I can defend to my board and to our security team.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: Of course. And that's exactly the kind of conversation I want to have. What — what are the specific things that would need to be true for you to feel confident going into a renewal?

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Well, a few things. One is obviously the reliability concern we just talked about. Two is — and this has been a slow-burn frustration for us — our compliance reporting situation. We are under PCI DSS obligations, we've got some HIPAA-adjacent data from our pharmacy locations, and right now generating those reports out of Aegis Comply is... it's manual, it's painful, it takes my team days to pull together what should be a few clicks.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: Okay, that's — that's actually really important feedback and I want to come back to that specifically because there's something relevant there. What's the third thing?

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: The third thing is that we've been talking to SentinelShield. And I want to be transparent about that because I think you deserve to know.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: I appreciate you telling me that. How far along are those conversations?

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: We've had two demos. We haven't asked for a formal proposal yet, but we're close. Their threat detection coverage looks solid and they made a pretty compelling case for their uptime SLAs, which given everything is — yeah, it's on our radar.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: Understood. And I'm not going to sit here and trash SentinelShield, that's not my style. But I do want to make sure you have the full picture on where we are and where we're going, because I think some of what you've described — especially on the compliance side — is actually about to change significantly.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: What do you mean?

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: So Aegis Comply v2 is launching on April 7th — which I know is after your renewal window opens, so I want to be upfront about that — but it includes on-demand reporting for PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2, and ISO 27001. Multi-framework, no manual aggregation, you can pull a report in real time. The workflow your team is dealing with today basically goes away.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: On-demand meaning... what exactly? Because I've heard 'on-demand' from vendors before and it means 'we queued your report and it'll be ready in four hours.'

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: Ha — fair. No, I mean genuinely on-demand. The data's already being ingested and mapped against the control frameworks continuously, so when you click generate, you're getting a live report. We're talking minutes, not hours. I can get you into a preview environment if that would help you evaluate it before the decision.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: That would actually be useful. My compliance lead has been pulling her hair out over this so — yeah, let's do that.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: Great, I'll set that up. Now I also — going back to the SentinelShield piece — I want to ask you something directly. Is the Detect outage the primary driver here or is it more of a cumulative frustration thing? Because those are kind of different problems.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Honestly? It's both. The outage was the thing that made this feel urgent. But if I'm being real with you, we've had some grumbles about support response times, about the Detect dashboard being a bit clunky for our team, and the compliance stuff has been a background annoyance for a while. So it all kind of... crystallized.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: That makes a lot of sense. And I don't want to dismiss any of those. The dashboard feedback — can you tell me more about what specifically feels clunky? Is it the alert triage flow, the filtering, or something else?

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Um, it's mostly the alert volume and the signal-to-noise ratio. We're getting flagged on things that are clearly not threats for our environment and then when something real comes through it can get buried. My SOC analyst — she actually flagged this to your support team back in January and I don't think she got a satisfying resolution.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: I'm going to pull that ticket after this call and personally follow up. That should not have been left unresolved for two months. I'm sorry about that.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: I'd appreciate that. Look, Aisha, I want to be clear — I'm not trying to be difficult here. I actually like working with you and I think the Aegis platform has genuinely helped us. But I have to be able to justify this to leadership and right now the story has some holes in it.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: I hear you. And I think that's completely fair. So let me propose this — I'll set up the Comply v2 preview with you and your compliance lead, I'll personally chase down that support ticket and get you a resolution, and I'd also like to put together a written summary of the Detect remediation steps with our updated SLA commitments so you have something concrete to bring to your CISO. Does that seem like a reasonable set of next steps?

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Yeah, that seems reasonable. I'd also want to have a conversation about whether there's any flexibility on the commercial side given... everything. I'm not saying that's a dealbreaker but it's something I'd need to at least explore.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: Absolutely, and I'll loop in my account executive on that piece. I want to make sure if we're talking numbers we're having the right conversation. I don't want to throw something out without being able to back it up properly.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: That works. Okay. I think — I came into this call pretty frustrated, so I'll give you credit, this was a more useful conversation than I expected.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: I'm glad. And Stephanie, genuinely — I want to earn the renewal, not just retain the contract. There's a difference and I take that seriously.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: I appreciate that. Alright, send me the calendar invite for the Comply preview and I'll get Sarah from our compliance team on it.

speaker: Aisha Johnson
sentence: Will do. I'll have something out to you by end of day Monday. Thanks again for being so direct with me today, it actually helps me help you.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Yep. Talk soon.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Evelyn, hi! Thanks so much for making time today, I know your calendar's been absolutely packed.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Maria, are you kidding? I've been looking forward to this one. We've got a lot to catch up on.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That's great to hear. And I also have Kevin O'Brien on the line with me today — he's one of our Solutions Architects, and he's been deep in the weeds on everything Comply-related, so I wanted to bring him in for this one.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Hey Evelyn, really glad to be here. I've been doing a lot of work with healthcare customers lately so hopefully I can add some useful context.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Perfect, love that. Kevin, welcome. And yeah, compliance has just been — it's been front and center for us this quarter, so the timing of this call is actually really good.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: So maybe let's just start there — what's been driving the urgency on the compliance side? Is it an upcoming audit, internal pressure, or...?

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: It's honestly a little bit of everything. We've got a HIPAA audit coming up in Q2, our board has been asking more pointed questions about our security posture, and um, we just onboarded two new hospital systems into our network so the scope of what we're responsible for has expanded significantly.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That's a lot of moving pieces all at once.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Yeah, it really is. And the honest truth is, our current compliance reporting process is — it's painful. We're still pulling together spreadsheets, chasing down evidence from like six different teams, and by the time we have a report ready it's already out of date.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Okay yeah, that's a really common pain point we hear, especially in healthcare orgs at your scale. Can I ask — are you currently using Aegis Comply at all, or is this more exploratory?

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: We are using it, we've been on it for about eight months now. We're using it primarily for SOC 2 right now, and it's been — honestly it's been a night and day difference from what we were doing before. But HIPAA specifically, that's where we feel like there are gaps.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: That makes a lot of sense. So the SOC 2 workflow is working well for you — that's great to hear. And the HIPAA gap, is that more about the framework mapping itself, or is it the evidence collection, or...?

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: It's kind of both, if I'm being honest. Like, I can see where controls overlap between SOC 2 and HIPAA in theory, but the platform right now makes me basically treat them as two completely separate workstreams. And that's just — that's not sustainable for my team.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Right, right. And that's actually — Kevin, do you want to jump in here? Because I think what Evelyn's describing is exactly what we've been building toward.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Yeah, absolutely. So Evelyn, this is something we've heard from a lot of customers in healthcare and financial services, and it's something the product team has been heads-down on. Without getting too far ahead of ourselves, what's coming in the Comply v2 roadmap is really designed to address exactly this — multi-framework support where a single piece of evidence can map across HIPAA, SOC 2, and other frameworks simultaneously.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Oh that's — okay, tell me more about that. Because if I can collect evidence once and have it satisfy controls across multiple frameworks, that would be... I mean that would be huge for us.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: That's exactly the idea. So instead of your team going to three different places for three different audits, you'd have one unified evidence library and the platform handles the cross-framework mapping for you.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: And I know you mentioned the board asking more questions too — one thing that's really exciting about where Comply is going is the reporting side. Like, being able to generate an on-demand report for a board meeting rather than spending a week pulling it together manually.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Oh gosh, yes. Our last board presentation, I kid you not, my team spent four days on the compliance section alone. Four days. That's time we're not spending on actual security work.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Yeah that's — that's a real cost, right? And it's hard to quantify sometimes but when you think about your team's time and attention, it adds up fast.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Totally. And honestly, one of the things I really appreciate about Aegis Comply versus what we looked at with some other vendors — I think VaultEdge was one — is that your framework actually integrates with our cloud environment natively. We're not just uploading PDFs and checking boxes.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: That's a really important distinction and I'm glad it's landing that way. The native integration piece is something we've invested in heavily, especially for healthcare customers where you've got such a complex environment — different EHR systems, cloud workloads, on-prem infrastructure...

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Exactly, yeah. We've got Epic, we've got Azure, we've got a couple of legacy on-prem systems that we're slowly migrating, so getting all of that into one compliance view has been... a journey.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: A journey, I love that. Very diplomatic. So, in terms of the HIPAA audit prep — when is the actual audit, do you know yet?

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: We're looking at late May, early June. So realistically we have about three months to get our evidence package together and make sure everything is airtight.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Okay, that's a really workable timeline actually. Kevin, from a technical standpoint, what would you recommend as the near-term focus for Evelyn's team given where they are with Comply today?

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Yeah, so a few things come to mind. First, I'd want to do a quick audit of how your current evidence library is structured for SOC 2, because a lot of what you've already built there will translate directly to HIPAA controls — we just need to make sure the tagging and mapping is set up correctly. Second, there are some HIPAA-specific controls around PHI access logging and audit trails that I'd want to make sure are being captured properly through your Detect integration.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: The Detect integration — yeah, that's been working really well actually. We've got our access logs flowing in and it's been much easier to demonstrate who accessed what and when, which auditors love.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: That's great, that's a really strong foundation. And then honestly, the third thing I'd say is — let's get you a sandbox look at some of the Comply v2 features before they go GA, just so your team can start thinking about how to reorganize your workflows. Maria, is that something we could arrange?

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Absolutely, yeah. Evelyn, I think we could set up a preview session — we've been doing that with a handful of strategic customers and the feedback has been really positive. Would that be useful for you?

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Yes, please. That would be — yeah, that's exactly what I want. Even just to be able to show my team what's coming so they can see there's a light at the end of the tunnel.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: I love that framing. Okay, so let me think about next steps — I'll follow up with you on scheduling that Comply v2 preview, and Kevin, can you send over a framework mapping document that shows the SOC 2 to HIPAA control overlap? Something Evelyn's team can actually use as a working guide.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Yeah, for sure. I'll put together a healthcare-specific version of that, pull in some of the PHI-related controls specifically. I'll have it to Maria by end of next week.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: That would be incredibly helpful. My compliance lead, she's going to be very happy when I forward that to her.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: And one more thing — Evelyn, you mentioned PCI earlier, or actually I think it was... wait, no, you didn't mention PCI. Sorry, I was thinking of another customer. Are PCI requirements on your radar at all or is it really just HIPAA and SOC 2?

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: No, PCI isn't really relevant for us on the clinical side. We do have some billing systems but those go through a third-party processor so we're mostly scoped out. HIPAA and SOC 2 are the big two.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Got it, good to know. Okay, I think we've covered a lot of ground today. Evelyn, any other questions or concerns you want to make sure we address before we wrap up?

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Honestly, no — I feel really good about where this is heading. I was a little nervous going into this call because Q2 feels like it's sneaking up on me, but talking through it, I think we've got a solid plan. I really appreciate you both making time.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Of course! That's what we're here for. And Evelyn, seriously, your team has done great work getting to where you are with the platform in eight months. A lot of organizations take much longer to get to this point.

speaker: Evelyn Park
sentence: Thank you, that means a lot. Alright, I'll keep an eye out for Kevin's framework doc and the preview session invite. Looking forward to it.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Perfect. We'll be in touch soon. Thanks everyone, take care!

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Thank you for holding, this is Elena Vasquez from Aegis Cloud Security senior support, I have your case pulled up here — am I speaking with Grace?

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Yes, Grace Yoon, and I also have Frank Delaney on the line, he's our VP of IT — Frank, you there?

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: Yeah I'm here. Elena, I'll be honest with you, I don't have a lot of patience left on this one.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: I completely understand, Frank, and I appreciate you both jumping on. I want to make sure we get to the bottom of this as quickly as possible. Can you walk me through what you're seeing on your end right now?

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: What we're seeing? What we're seeing is nothing, Elena. That's the whole problem. Our Detect dashboard has been showing no events, no alerts, nothing since — Grace, when did it start?

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: We first noticed it around 6 AM this morning, March 15th. The event feed just... stopped. No incoming threat events, the monitoring panel looks completely dark. And we are a pharmaceutical company, we are subject to HIPAA, we have patient data, and we have zero visibility into our threat landscape right now.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Right, so I do need to be upfront with you both — and I want to apologize because you absolutely should have received direct communication about this already. Aegis Detect is currently experiencing a platform-wide outage affecting the event processing pipeline. This is not isolated to Northstar, it's impacting multiple customers.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: A platform-wide outage. So this isn't something on our end.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: No, it is not on your end at all. The root cause is on Aegis's infrastructure side, specifically the event ingestion pipeline.

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: Okay so let me — let me just make sure I understand this correctly. We are paying for a security monitoring product. In healthcare. With HIPAA obligations. And your infrastructure just... fails? And nobody called us? Nobody sent an email? Grace found out because she happened to check the dashboard this morning?

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Frank, you are absolutely right to be angry about that. The communication failure on our part is inexcusable and I'm not going to try to defend it. I've already flagged your account as priority escalation and our incident communications team is aware that enterprise healthcare customers were not notified in a timely manner.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: How long has this been going on? Because if it started at 6 AM that's already — it's almost 10 now.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: The outage actually began earlier than that, around midnight Pacific. So we're looking at roughly... yes, approximately nine to ten hours at this point with no event processing.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Nine hours. Nine hours of no threat monitoring. Elena, do you understand what that means for us? We are in the middle of a SOC 2 audit cycle. We have to demonstrate continuous monitoring. If there's a gap like this — a nine hour gap — that is a finding. That could affect our audit outcome.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: I hear you Grace and I want to address the audit concern directly because that's really important. Our engineering team is aware of the compliance implications and we will be providing detailed incident documentation that you can use as part of your audit record — timestamps, root cause, remediation steps, the whole thing.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: That's — okay that's something but it doesn't fix the nine hour gap in our logs.

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: What's the ETA on resolution? Give me a number.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Our engineering team's current estimate is two to four hours for full event processing restoration. I want to be transparent that estimate has already slipped once this morning, so I don't want to give you a hard guarantee, but that is the latest from the incident team as of about thirty minutes ago.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: It's slipped once already. Fantastic. Frank, I told you this morning, I told you we should have had a redundancy conversation with them months ago.

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: Yeah. Yeah you did. Elena, I'm going to be very direct with you. We have been with Aegis for two and a half years. We expanded our contract last fall to include Protect and Identity on top of Detect. We are not a small account. And I am sitting here right now looking at alternatives. SentinelShield, CyberNova — I've had calls with both of them in the last six months.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Frank, I hear that, and I'm not going to try to talk you out of that evaluation during a live outage — that wouldn't be appropriate. What I can tell you is that I am personally committed to making sure your account gets the attention it needs starting right now. I'm going to stay on this with you until we have resolution.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: What does that actually mean practically? What are you doing right now for us?

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Right now I have your ticket flagged as P1 executive escalation, I've looped in your account team so your CSM is aware, and I am actively in the incident bridge with our engineering team. I can provide you both a direct update every thirty minutes via email or I can keep you on a separate bridge line — whichever is more useful for you.

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: Email updates every thirty minutes, fine. But Grace and I also need to know — is there any interim workaround? Any way to get even partial visibility into what's happening in our environment while the pipeline is down?

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: So the event display in the dashboard is fully dependent on the processing pipeline, there's no way to surface raw events independently right now. What I can tell you is that the agents on your endpoints are still collecting data locally — none of that telemetry is being lost, it's queued. When the pipeline comes back online it will backfill. So you won't have a permanent gap in your data, just a delay in visibility.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Okay that's — that's actually something I didn't know. So the data is there, it's just not being processed yet.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Correct. The telemetry is queued and will be backfilled. I can confirm that in writing as part of the incident documentation.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: That helps for the audit log argument but it still doesn't help us right now if there's an active threat in our environment that nobody is catching.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: That's a fair point and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. The honest answer is that for the duration of this outage, real-time alerting is not functioning. If you have a separate SIEM or any other network monitoring in place those would be your fallback right now.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: We have a basic SIEM but we deprioritized tuning it because we were relying on Detect. Which now feels like a mistake.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: I understand. After this incident is resolved I want to make sure we schedule a proper architecture review with your team — redundancy, failover posture, how Detect fits into your broader stack. That conversation is overdue and that's on us.

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: Elena, I want to ask you point blank — is this a systemic infrastructure problem with Aegis or is this a one-time incident? Because if your event ingestion has a single point of failure baked into the architecture, I need to know that.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Frank, I'm going to be straight with you. The root cause does involve a single point of failure in the event ingestion layer. That is what engineering has identified. I am not going to tell you this is a one-time freak occurrence — the architecture had a vulnerability and it failed. The remediation plan includes redundant processing nodes and changes to the ingestion architecture, but I can't promise you that work is already done.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: I appreciate the honesty. I do. But Frank, you're hearing this right — single point of failure in a security product.

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: Yeah. Elena, I want a call with your VP of Engineering and our CSM within 48 hours of this incident being resolved. Not a support ticket, not an email chain — a call. With someone who can speak to the architectural remediation roadmap.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: That is a completely reasonable request and I will personally make sure that is scheduled. I'm documenting that right now in your case. You'll have a confirmation from your CSM before end of business today.

speaker: Frank Delaney
sentence: Alright. Thirty minute email updates, incident documentation for the audit, and that executive call within 48 hours of resolution. If we don't see movement on the pipeline in the next two hours we're going to have a much harder conversation.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Understood. I have all of that noted and I'm on it. Grace, is there anything else you need from me right now before I get back into the incident bridge?

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Just — please make sure the incident documentation explicitly covers the compliance gap. I need it to be detailed enough to present to an auditor. Timeline, what failed, what data was preserved. All of it.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Absolutely. I'll make sure the documentation is audit-ready. You have my direct line — if anything changes on your end or you need to reach me before the next update, do not hesitate. I'm so sorry we're in this situation and I'm going to do everything I can to get you through it.

speaker: Grace Yoon
sentence: Okay. Thank you Elena. We'll be watching our inbox.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Alright, I think we're all on now — Charles, can you hear us okay?

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Yeah, I'm here. Audio's fine on my end.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Great. And Kevin just joined too, so we're all set. Charles, thanks for making time — I know this week has probably been, uh, a lot for your team.

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Yeah, 'a lot' is one way to put it, Maria. It's been a rough few days, honestly.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Completely understood. And that's exactly why we wanted to get on a call sooner rather than later — not just to send an email but actually, you know, sit down and walk through what happened and where we go from here.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Kevin, you want to kick us off with the technical summary?

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Sure, yeah. So, Charles, first — I just want to say I'm sorry. Your team should not have experienced what happened, and I want to be straightforward with you about the root cause. What we had was essentially a single point of failure in our event ingestion pipeline. One component went down and, uh, it cascaded. It took the whole processing layer with it.

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Right, so for — what was it, six hours? — we had zero threat monitoring visibility. None. Our security team was flying blind.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: That's correct. Six hours, approximately. And I — yeah, there's no softening that. That's a significant window.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Kevin, can you tell him what the fix looks like? Because I think that's important context.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Yeah, absolutely. So we've already implemented redundant processing nodes — so there's no longer a single point of failure in that ingestion layer. And we've also put a circuit breaker pattern in place, which means if one node starts degrading, it gets isolated before it can take down the rest of the pipeline. This is already in production as of this morning.

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Okay. And I appreciate you explaining that, Kevin, I do. But — and I want to be honest with you both here — that doesn't fully address my concern. The concern isn't just what caused it. It's that it happened at all, and that we didn't know it was happening until almost two hours in.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That's a fair point, Charles.

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Two hours before we got any notification. My team was the one who flagged it to your support desk. We noticed our dashboards had gone stale before Aegis even sent us an alert. That's — I mean, that's backwards. A threat monitoring product should be monitoring itself.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: You're absolutely right, and that's — honestly, that's the part that stings the most from our side too. The alerting on the internal failure was not working the way it should have. That's a gap we've identified and it's being addressed alongside the pipeline fix.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Charles, can I ask — and I want to understand the full picture here — what was the actual impact on Helix during that window? Did anything slip through, or was it more of an exposure risk that you had to manage?

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Fortunately — and I say fortunately with a lot of emphasis there — nothing slipped through that we've found. But we spent, I don't know, twelve, fifteen person-hours doing a manual review of logs after the fact just to confirm that. My team basically had to do Aegis Detect's job manually for six hours and then audit it afterward. That's not a small thing for us.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Yeah, that's — yeah. That's a real cost and I don't want to minimize it.

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: And look, I like you guys. I've always liked working with your team. But I have to be transparent — this isn't happening in a vacuum. We've been having internal conversations about our security stack, and, um, we've been talking to VaultEdge as part of a broader vendor review. I'm not saying that to be threatening, I just — you should know where we're at.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: I appreciate you telling us that, Charles. I really do. Can you tell us a bit more about what's driving that review — is it primarily this incident, or were there concerns before?

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Honestly? It's both. This incident accelerated the conversation, but we've had some lower-level grumbles for a while. The alert noise from Detect has been higher than we'd like — a lot of false positives that my team has to triage. And the custom rule configuration, it's... it's more cumbersome than it should be for a platform at this price point.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: The false positive rate — is that something you've flagged with support previously, or has it mostly been absorbed internally?

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Mostly absorbed internally, which I'll admit is partly on us for not escalating sooner. But there's also an expectation that a product this mature should have better out-of-the-box tuning, you know?

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: Yeah, that's fair feedback and I want to make sure we capture that properly. On the rule configuration side — Kevin, is that something we can get eyes on specifically for Helix's environment?

speaker: Kevin O'Brien
sentence: Absolutely. Charles, I'd actually like to set up a separate working session just on the detection tuning — look at what rules you have configured, where the noise is coming from, and get you to a better baseline. That's something we can do in the next week or two.

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: I mean, I'm open to that. I just want to make sure it's not a band-aid. Like, if the underlying product has structural issues, tuning sessions aren't going to change my view on the platform.

speaker: Maria Santos
sentence: That's completely reasonable and I hear you. I don't want to oversell a tuning session either. What I'd want to understand better is — when you're talking to VaultEdge, what is it that they're showing you that's resonating? Because I want to understand if there are genuine capability gaps on our side.

speaker: Charles Ng
sentence: Uh, well — their detection pipeline architecture, from what they've shown us, is distributed by design. Which, in light of what just happened, is obviously appealing. They've also been talking about their alert correlation engine, which is supposedly a lot better at suppressing redundant alerts. We haven't done a full POC yet so I can't say for sure how it actually performs.

speaker: Kevin O'Brien

sentence: You're right, and I — I want to own that, the initial response time on this case wasn't where it should've been and I'm sorry about that. speaker: David Kim
sentence: Okay, and are all of your backup jobs affected or is it specific workloads — like, is it just certain VMs, certain datasets, or is this across the board?

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: That's a good question. It seems like it's — it's pretty broad, but the worst offenders are our SQL Server instances. We've got three pretty beefy SQL servers running our e-commerce database and our inventory management system, and those are the ones really blowing past the window. Some of the smaller file server backups are also slow but not as dramatic.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Got it, so SQL workloads are the primary pain point. And are these incremental backups that are running nightly or are you doing full backups every night?

sentence: Right, yeah, incrementals ballooning like that is definitely a red flag. A few things I want to check — have you seen any significant growth in your data change rate? speaker: David Kim

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Go ahead, yeah.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Okay, so I'm looking at your environment here... and yeah, I can see the job durations, oof — yeah that's significant. Your SQL backup for, looks like it's the instance labeled SQLPROD01, that one's averaging about eight hours over the last ten days. That's uh, that's not good.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Right, exactly! And I keep getting told by my team to just expand the window but that's not a real fix, I shouldn't have to carve out more production hours for backups that used to run in under two hours.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: No, absolutely not, that's a band-aid, not a solution. Let me look at the throughput metrics here... okay so I'm seeing the backup throughput dropped pretty substantially — it looks like you were averaging around 800 megabytes per second before March 27th and now you're sitting around 180 to 220. That's a massive drop in throughput.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: So something changed on your end? Because we didn't change anything on our infrastructure around that time, I specifically asked my team about that.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I hear you, and I want to be transparent — so looking at our platform logs, around March 10th through the 18th we had a significant incident with Aegis Detect, our threat monitoring pipeline, and as part of the remediation work we pushed some backend infrastructure updates. Now, Protect and Detect run on separate systems, but some of the underlying network routing configurations were touched as part of that work, and I'm wondering if — and I need to confirm this with our infrastructure team — if some of those changes affected the data transfer paths for backup jobs in your region.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Wait, so a problem with a completely different module potentially broke our backups and nobody flagged this to customers? That's — I mean, I'm sorry David but that's pretty alarming to me.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: I understand your frustration, Dominic, and I'm not going to spin this — if that's what happened, it shouldn't have happened that way and customers should have been proactively notified. speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Okay. I — I appreciate that, at least. So what are the next steps here? Because I need this fixed before tonight, we cannot have another morning where our warehouse team is fighting with a sluggish inventory system because backups are still running.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Okay so here's what I can do right now — I can escalate this internally to our Protect infrastructure team with the throughput data I'm looking at, and I also want to check your backup agent version because I'm seeing you're running 4.1.2, and we released 4.2.0 about three weeks ago which had some improvements to the data transfer compression algorithms. sentence: Why wasn't that update pushed automatically? We're on managed updates.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: That's a fair question — 4.2.0 was staged as an opt-in update rather than automatic because of some compatibility flags for SQL Server environments, which is ironic given your situation. But let me get that queued up for your environment, I'll push it out as a manual deployment through the console and you should see it applied within the hour if you can confirm your agents are reachable.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: They should be, yeah. And that update — is that going to fix the throughput issue or is that just going to help a little bit?

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Honest answer? It should help, probably meaningfully, but I don't want to promise you it's the full fix until I understand whether there's a network routing issue on our backend. I'm going to get the infra team on this today — I'm flagging it as a P1 on my end — and I want to schedule a follow-up call with you tomorrow morning, before your backup window starts, so we can walk through whatever findings we have.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Tomorrow morning works. And I want someone from your engineering team on that call, not just — no offense — not just a support engineer. I need someone who can actually make changes on the backend if needed.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: None taken, I'll make that happen. I'll have a Protect platform engineer on the call. Can we say 10 AM your time? And what timezone are you in?

sentence: We're Central, so 10 AM Central works.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Perfect, I'll send a calendar invite to the email on your account within the next 30 minutes. For example, I can look at adjusting the backup job priority settings and throttling some of the lower-priority file server jobs so the SQL workloads get more of the available bandwidth during the window.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Yeah, yeah, let's do that. The SQL servers are the priority, the file servers we can live with being a little slower if it means the SQL jobs finish closer to on time.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Okay, I'm going to make those priority adjustments now in your policy configuration, and combined with the agent update that should — hopefully — get tonight's run into a better place. I'll also set up an alert so if the SQL job duration exceeds six hours I get paged directly, and I'll check in on it tomorrow morning before our call.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Alright. I mean, look, I'm still frustrated that we're two weeks into this and I'm just now getting real traction, but — I do appreciate you being straight with me about what you're seeing and not just giving me the runaround. Let's see what tomorrow brings.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: That's fair, Dominic, and I get it. We'll make this right. I'll have that invite and a case update email to you within the half hour, and I'll see you tomorrow at 10 Central with the engineering team.

sentence: Sounds good. Talk to you then.

sentence: Thank you Dominic, talk soon.

sentence: Hi, thanks for joining — this is Sarah Chen from Aegis Cloud Security support, and I also have my colleague Elena Vasquez on the line with me today. Elena specializes in our Identity module so I wanted to bring her in given what you described in the case notes.

sentence: Great, yeah. This is Stephanie Blair, VP of Technology at Nova Retail Group, and I have Andre Coleman on with me — he's one of our senior security engineers and he's been in the thick of this since it started.

sentence: Hi everyone, Andre here. Yeah, happy to be on — well, not happy about the situation, but you know what I mean.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Totally understand, and hi Andre, hi Stephanie. We've reviewed the case notes you submitted but I'd love to just hear directly from you — can you walk us through what you're experiencing? When did this start?

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: So it started — Andre, was it Tuesday morning? Around like 9 AM?

speaker: Andre Coleman
sentence: Yeah, Tuesday February 10th, roughly 8:50, 8:55 AM central time. We started getting a flood of tickets from users saying they couldn't log in through SSO. Something like 'authentication failed, please contact your administrator.'

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: And this is affecting — I want to make sure I understand the scope — is this all of your users or a subset?

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: That's what's so frustrating about this, honestly. It's not all users. It's roughly — Andre, what are we at, like 40 percent?

speaker: Andre Coleman
sentence: Yeah, we've been tracking it. About 38 to 42 percent of our user base is hitting the failure. We have around 1,200 users in Aegis Identity and somewhere between 450 and 500 of them are completely locked out. speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Okay, that's really helpful context. And just to confirm — you're using Okta as your identity provider, correct? That's what's in the case.

sentence: That's right, Okta. SAML 2.0. We've been running this configuration for, what, eight, nine months without any issues, and then Tuesday just — boom.

sentence: And nothing changed on your Okta side? No config updates, no certificate renewals, anything like that?

sentence: Nothing. I went through everything. Our Okta config is untouched. The SAML app settings are exactly what they've been. I even pulled the metadata and compared it to what we had exported three weeks ago — identical.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Okay so, and I want to be transparent here — we do have some reports from a couple of other customers about SSO intermittency, and our engineering team is actively investigating. I don't want to say definitively this is a platform bug yet, but it's something we're taking seriously. Elena, do you want to talk about what we've been looking at on our end?

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Yeah, absolutely. So um, what we've identified so far is there may be an issue with how Aegis Identity is processing certain SAML assertion attributes — specifically around the NameID format. There was a minor update pushed to the Identity module on February 9th, which was the day before you started seeing issues, and we think it may have introduced a regression in how we're handling persistent versus transient NameID formats.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Wait — there was an update pushed the day before this started and you're just telling us this now? We've had 500 users locked out for four days.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: You're absolutely right to be frustrated by that, Stephanie, and I'm sorry. The update was a minor patch — it wasn't expected to affect SSO behavior and it didn't trigger our standard pre-release SSO regression tests the way it should have. That's a gap in our process and I don't want to gloss over that.

speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: I mean, look — I get it, things happen. But four days is a long time. We've got retail operations staff who literally cannot access the security platform. speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Understood, and that context is really important — thank you for sharing that. speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: We did try that for a handful of users. The problem is we enforced SSO-only as a security requirement, so local auth is disabled for most of our user accounts. Andre, is that still the case across the board?

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Right, okay. So Elena, can we talk about the admin override option? Because I think that might be relevant here.

sentence: Yes — so Stephanie, Andre, there is an option in the Aegis Identity admin console where a super admin can temporarily re-enable local authentication on a per-user basis without changing your global policy. speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Okay, that's... actually useful. Andre, can you look at that while we're on the call?

sentence: Yeah, pulling it up now. Identity Settings... User Management... okay I see it. Authentication Override, yeah. So I'd have to do this for each of the 450 affected users individually?

speaker: Andre Coleman
sentence: Okay, yeah, I see the bulk action. That's... manageable. Not great, but manageable. I'll take it.

sentence: Good. And I want to be clear that this is a stopgap — we are working on a proper fix. sentence: I'd also like to understand — are there any other customers affected by this? Because if this is a widespread issue I'd expect a status page update or some kind of proactive communication, and I haven't seen anything.

sentence: That's a fair point. speaker: Stephanie Blair
sentence: Yeah. Yeah, exactly. Look, I want to be clear — we're not going anywhere, we're committed to Aegis, but this has been a rough week and I need to see some responsiveness here. Our CISO is asking me questions I don't have great answers to right now.

speaker: Elena Vasquez
sentence: Completely understood. I want to make sure you have everything you need to communicate internally. Would it help if we drafted a brief incident summary email that you could share with your CISO? Something that lays out the root cause, what we know, and the remediation plan?

sentence: Actually, yeah. That would be helpful. Something I can forward that doesn't come from me trying to paraphrase a support call.

sentence: Sure, so the concern was basically — if a customer kicks off, say, a SOC 2 report on demand, how long does that take and is the UX acceptable? sentence: Alright, I think we're all on — can everyone hear me okay?

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Okay good, so — let's just dive in because I don't want this to run long. The reason I called this is, uh, we've been getting some pretty alarming signals from the Protect module over the last couple of weeks and I think we need to talk through what's actually happening before this becomes a customer-facing issue.

sentence: Yeah, I'm glad we're finally having this conversation honestly. I've been flagging some of these benchmark numbers for like three weeks now and it feels like nothing's moved.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Okay, let's not — I hear you Nina, and I want to get to that, but let's just start with the actual data so we're all on the same page. Tyler, you ran the load tests last week, can you walk us through what you saw?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Sure, yeah. So we ran a simulated backup workload at roughly 2x our current P95 customer scale, and the results were... not great. Throughput started degrading at around the 60% load mark, and by the time we hit full simulated load we were seeing backup job queue times spike to like — I think the worst number was 47 minutes for a job that should complete in under five.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: 47 minutes? I — sorry, did you say 47?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Yeah. 47. And that's not even worst-case, that's just what we observed in the test window. It could be worse under sustained load.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: That's... I mean that's genuinely bad. Like that's not a tuning problem, that's a fundamental architecture concern.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Right, which is sort of what I've been trying to say. The scheduler — and look, I don't want to throw anyone under the bus here — but the job scheduler isn't built to handle concurrent dispatch at this scale. It was designed for, I don't know, a much smaller customer footprint than where we're heading.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Okay and this is exactly what I documented in QA-1847 back in, uh, late February. The concurrency ceiling on the scheduler queue was a known risk item. I flagged it as high severity.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Nina, I know you flagged it. It was on the backlog. We were dealing with — look, we had the Detect outage in March, that pulled a lot of engineering bandwidth, and some things got deprioritized. That's just the reality.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: I understand that, Chris, and I'm not trying to relitigate prioritization decisions. But I think we have to acknowledge that we knew about this and we're now sitting here looking at 47-minute queue times. That's... that's uncomfortable.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: It is uncomfortable. I'm not disagreeing. Let's focus on what we do about it.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: So I've been doing some digging since Tyler shared the numbers yesterday, and I think there are actually two separate issues tangled together here. One is the scheduler concurrency problem which Nina flagged, and the other is — and this one I actually didn't expect — the snapshot deduplication layer is doing a lot of synchronous I/O that it really shouldn't be. Like it's blocking on disk reads mid-job.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Oh, yeah — I actually saw that too in the traces. There's this dedup check that happens inline and it's just... it's not async. It never was.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: So that's compounding the queue problem. Because jobs are blocking longer than they should, which backs up the queue, which makes the scheduler worse.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: It's like, it's a pressure cooker situation. Each problem makes the other one worse. Great.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Okay, so realistically — what are we looking at in terms of effort to fix the scheduler concurrency issue? Sofia, you know that codebase better than anyone.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Honestly? It's not a quick fix. The scheduler wasn't designed to be horizontally scalable. To do this properly we'd probably need to refactor the dispatch layer, introduce some kind of distributed queue — I keep thinking something like a partitioned queue model would actually solve this — but we're talking weeks of work. Plural. Not two weeks, more like... six to eight minimum if we want to do it right and not create new problems.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Six to eight weeks. Okay. And we're heading into what's supposed to be a growth quarter for Protect. Sales has been pushing it pretty hard post the Comply v2 launch — they want to bundle them together.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Which is great for the business but if we scale the customer base and the infrastructure can't handle it, we're going to have outages. Real ones. Customer-impacting ones.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: And that's the last thing we need right now. After March, after the Detect thing — honestly our credibility with some of these enterprise customers is already fragile. Another major incident would be... it would not be good.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Yeah, I think about that a lot. The Detect outage — the six hours of no visibility — that shook some people. I know we fixed it, the redundant nodes, the circuit breaker stuff, all of that. But trust takes a while to rebuild and piling a Protect failure on top of that is not something I want to explain to leadership.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: So what do we do in the meantime? Like is there a stopgap? Because six to eight weeks for a full refactor doesn't help us if we're onboarding new customers next month.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: There might be some things we can do at the config level. Like we could tighten the job concurrency limits per tenant — basically throttle how many concurrent backup jobs any single customer can run — which buys us some headroom on the queue. It's not a fix, it's a band-aid, but it could prevent the worst-case blowups.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Yeah but then we're artificially limiting the product for customers who are paying for, you know, enterprise-grade backup. That feels wrong.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: It does feel wrong. But the alternative might be an outage, which is worse.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: I agree with Sofia. A graceful degradation we control is better than an uncontrolled failure. We implement the throttle as a temporary measure, we communicate it internally, and we stay off the critical path while we do the real fix.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: And the async dedup issue — is that more tractable? Like can we decouple that faster than the scheduler work?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Yeah, actually I think the dedup thing is more isolated. If we move the deduplication check to an async worker and just do a quick hash lookup first with a fallback... I mean I'd need to prototype it but I think that's a one to two week effort. Maybe less.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: Okay, that's at least something. That's one concrete thing we can actually move on quickly.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Can I ask something that might be uncomfortable? Do we need to be having a conversation about whether sales should be slowing down Protect commitments right now? Like, should we be having a conversation with them about customer onboarding pace?

sentence: Ugh. I mean — yes, probably. That's going to be a fun conversation. Sales is riding high on the Comply v2 momentum and they're not going to love being told to pump the brakes on Protect.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: I know, but better to have that conversation now than to have it after a customer's backups fail during a ransomware event or something. Like, that's the scenario we're trying to avoid.

speaker: Chris Lee
sentence: You're right. Okay. I'll set up time with the sales lead this week. I'll frame it as a scaling preparation conversation rather than a stop-selling conversation. But the message will basically be: let's be thoughtful about onboarding pace for the next six to eight weeks.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: That's probably the right framing. And in parallel, Tyler and I can start sketching out the scheduler architecture? Like even just getting the design doc started so we know what we're actually committing to?

sentence: Yeah, let's do that. I can pull together the load test traces and annotate them so you've got the full picture of where things are falling apart.

sentence: Absolutely, and I take that very seriously, I do. Can you walk me through what you're seeing on your end? The case notes mention an API integration error with Aegis Protect but I want to hear it directly from you.

sentence: So we have our EHR system — it's a custom-built platform — integrated with Aegis Protect through your REST API, and starting Sunday we started getting 503 errors on the backup initiation endpoint. Specifically the... um, it's the /v2/backup/jobs/create endpoint.

sentence: Okay, the /v2/backup/jobs/create endpoint, got it. And are these errors consistent or intermittent? Like are they happening on every call or—

sentence: Every single call. One hundred percent failure rate since Sunday morning, approximately 6 AM our time, which is Pacific. We have zero successful backup jobs since then.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Zero successful jobs in three days, okay, that's — yeah, that's significant. And are you seeing anything in the response body with the 503? Any error message or is it just the status code?

sentence: We get a very unhelpful message that says 'service temporarily unavailable, please retry' — which we have been retrying, with exponential backoff as your documentation specifies, and it hasn't made a difference.

sentence: Okay, and what authentication method are you using? Bearer token, API key, or—

sentence: Bearer token, OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow. And before you ask — yes, the tokens are valid, yes they're being refreshed properly, we're not hitting any 401s or 403s, the 503s start immediately after auth succeeds.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: That's — that's actually really helpful context, thank you. So authentication is working fine, it's specifically the job creation call that's failing. Okay. Can I ask, do you have a request ID from any of the failed calls? That would help me trace it on our backend.

speaker: Dr. Linda Cheng
sentence: I have several. I'll read you one — it's X-Request-ID: ae7f2c-b339-48d1-9cf2-7744d0a8e312. My team logged about two hundred of them over the past three days.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Perfect, let me pull that up... okay, I'm in our trace logs now. Give me just a second here.

speaker: Dr. Linda Cheng
sentence: And while you're doing that, I want to raise something else — we noticed that when we try to manually trigger backups through the Aegis Protect web console, those also fail. So this doesn't seem like it's purely an API integration issue on our side, this looks like something is broken on your end.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Oh, that's — that's a really important data point, the console failures too. That does shift the picture significantly. Okay so I'm looking at this request ID and I can see... hm. Okay. So it looks like the job is hitting our backup orchestration service and it's timing out before it can queue. Let me see what's upstream from that.

sentence: What does that mean in plain English? Because I need to understand whether this is something my team caused or something on Aegis's end, because right now my CTO position — I'm the one responsible for data integrity and I need to give my board an explanation.

sentence: I understand, and I want to be transparent with you. What I'm seeing in the logs is that the backup orchestration layer is rejecting job submissions, and it's not a client-side error — this is happening at our infrastructure level. This is not something your team did wrong.

speaker: Dr. Linda Cheng
sentence: Okay, so Aegis broke something. And it took three days and a support call for someone to tell me that.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: Dr. Cheng, I — I hear you, and that is a fair criticism. We should have proactive alerting that catches this for affected customers and reaches out. I'm going to escalate this right now to our Protect infrastructure team while we're on this call.

speaker: Dr. Linda Cheng
sentence: Good. And in the meantime, what are my options? Because we have a — we have a compliance window coming up, we need to demonstrate backup continuity for our HIPAA audit, and right now I cannot do that.

speaker: Priya Patel
sentence: So there are a couple of things we can do right now. sentence: Okay. How quickly can that happen?

sentence: I can get that initiated within the next hour — I'll coordinate with our Protect ops team directly. sentence: Four hours. Okay. And I want to be clear — I'm not just frustrated about the technical issue, I'm frustrated about the communication. Three days of radio silence from Aegis is not acceptable for a healthcare customer. We are handling protected health information.

sentence: You're completely right, and I'm not going to make excuses for that. The lack of proactive communication on our end was a failure, and I'm going to make sure that's fed back to our leadership and that you receive a formal apology along with that incident report.

sentence: Okay, good — that actually helps narrow down the scope. speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Okay, I think everyone's on now — Tyler, can you hear us okay?

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Yeah, yeah I'm here, sorry I was — had to grab the right headset, my other one died.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Alright, let's just get into it because honestly we've been in firefighting mode for the past four days and I want to actually understand what happened here.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Yeah, agreed. So, Mike, do you want to walk us through what you found? You were the one digging into the pipeline logs last night.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Sure, yeah. So, um, the short version is — we had a cascading failure in the event ingestion layer. There's essentially a single point of failure in how we're routing events through the processing pipeline, and once that node went down on Monday morning, everything backed up.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Wait, a single point of failure? Like, we've known about that risk for how long? I feel like that was flagged in Q3 last year.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: It was flagged, yeah. It was in the backlog. It just... never got prioritized above the feature work.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: That is honestly really frustrating to hear, Mike, because we had six hours — six hours — where customers had zero threat monitoring visibility. Like, Detect is supposed to be the thing that tells you when something bad is happening.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: I know. I know, and I'm not trying to make excuses. The prioritization call was made above our level and at the time there were reasons, but obviously in hindsight—

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: In hindsight it was the wrong call, yeah.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Can we talk about the actual failure sequence though? Like I want to understand technically what cascaded. Was it a memory issue, was it network, what actually triggered the initial node failure?

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: So from what I can tell in the logs, the primary ingestion node started throwing out-of-memory errors around 6:47 AM. And instead of failing gracefully, it just — it locked up. And then the queue started building, and because there was no circuit breaker, the downstream processors kept trying to pull from a source that wasn't responding.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Right, and that's what caused the cascading part. The downstream processors themselves started degrading.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Exactly. And by the time our alerting caught it — which was, ugh, almost 40 minutes later — the whole pipeline was basically dead.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Forty minutes before alerting kicked in? Why did it take that long? We have health checks on those services.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: The health checks were pinging the node endpoint, which was still technically responding — it just wasn't processing anything. So the check was returning healthy even though the service was completely stuck.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Oh, that's — okay, so the health check was essentially useless in this scenario.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Pretty much, yeah. It was a liveness check, not a readiness check. Which is a gap we clearly need to fix.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Okay so we've got at least three things here — the single point of failure in ingestion, no circuit breaker pattern, and inadequate health checking. Is that a fair summary so far?

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah, that's how I'd characterize it. And I think the fix for the ingestion layer specifically is redundant processing nodes — at minimum two, probably three, with active load balancing.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: And the circuit breaker — like, how complicated is that to implement? I don't want us to say 'we'll implement a circuit breaker' and then that also ends up sitting in the backlog for a year.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: It's not trivial but it's also not like a three-month project. I think we could have a working implementation in two, maybe two and a half weeks if we actually focus on it.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: I want that estimate in writing honestly, because I've heard 'two weeks' before and then it becomes six.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: That's fair, Nina, that's fair. I'll put together a proper scope doc today.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: What about the communication side of this? Like, customers were in the dark for hours. Do we know how that was handled?

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: That's — yeah, that's a whole other conversation, but I will say the status page update was way too slow. That's something that needs to be escalated separately.

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Agreed. And from a QA perspective, I'm also really bothered by the fact that we didn't have a test scenario for this kind of failure. Like, do we even have chaos engineering coverage on the ingestion pipeline?

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: We don't. Not really. We have some basic load testing but nothing that simulates a node going unresponsive.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Yeah, that needs to change. Like, we should be deliberately killing nodes in staging on a regular basis to validate that the system handles it.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: One hundred percent. I'm going to push for that as part of the remediation plan. It won't just be 'fix the code' — it needs to be 'build the testing practice too.'

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Good. And honestly, uh, I think we need to be really transparent in the post-mortem doc about the fact that this risk was known and deprioritized. I don't think we should soften that.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: I agree with that. If we're going to learn from this, we have to be honest about the decision-making chain that got us here, not just the technical failure.

speaker: Mike Romano
sentence: Yeah. It's uncomfortable but it's the right call.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Okay, so action items — Mike is doing the scope doc for the circuit breaker and redundant nodes, Ananya and Tyler are looking at the health check improvements, and Nina, can you start scoping out what a chaos testing framework would look like for the pipeline?

speaker: Nina Kowalski
sentence: Yeah, I can do that. I'll probably want to pull in Tyler for part of it.

speaker: Tyler Washington
sentence: Works for me.

speaker: Sofia Petrov
sentence: Alright. This has been a really rough week and I don't think anyone feels great about where we're at, but — let's make sure this doesn't happen again. I'll circulate the post-mortem draft by end of day tomorrow.

speaker: Ananya Sharma
sentence: Thanks everyone.

sentence: I — yes, Lauren, I understand completely why that is frustrating and I'm not going to try to spin that, what I can tell you is I have our on-call engineering team paged right now and we are treating this as a P1 incident.

sentence: A P1 incident, great, what does that mean in terms of actual resolution time? Because I need to give my security team something, they're sitting there right now with no visibility and I need a number.

sentence: I can't give you a hard ETA right now because I'm still diagnosing the root cause, but what I can tell you is that our P1 SLA commits our engineering team to active remediation within fifteen minutes of escalation, and I've already triggered that.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: Okay but that fifteen minutes started when, right now? Because my team has already been down for almost an hour.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: The escalation was triggered just now, yes, and — and I want to be transparent with you about that, Lauren, I'm not going to pretend otherwise.

speaker: Lauren Bishop
sentence: David, I'm going to be really honest with you right now — we have been evaluating SentinelShield as an alternative for the past couple of months, and this, this right here is exactly the kind of thing that makes that decision very easy.

speaker: David Kim
sentence: Lauren, I — I hear that, and I understand, and I'm not going to beg you to stay on a call like this, what I'm going to do is focus on getting your service restored and making sure our team follows up with a full incident report and a conversation with your account team about what happened.

sentence: Is there anything — and I know this is a bad situation — is there anything on your end, like any cached data or any local logging that your team is using in the interim while we work on restoring the feed?

sentence: We have our own endpoint logs but that's not — that's not what we're paying you for, David, we're paying for centralized threat detection and correlation, not to go dig through raw logs manually, my team is not equipped to do that in real time.

sentence: Yeah, no, I understand, I wasn't suggesting that as a solution, more just trying to understand your current exposure level while we work on this.

sentence: Our exposure level is — it's unknown, David, that's the whole problem, I don't know what's happening on our network right now and that is not acceptable for a company like ours.

sentence: I'm getting an update from our incident channel right now — it looks like the engineering team has identified a cascading failure in the event processing pipeline, they're working on isolating the affected component.

sentence: Hundreds? Like, what's your normal alert volume on a Saturday?

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: Maybe fifteen, twenty alerts on a slow day. We're a retail company, we have a lot of endpoints, but our baseline is pretty well established at this point. So going from twenty to, I don't know, I counted over three hundred by 9 AM — that's not normal.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: And our on-call person spent the entire morning triaging and every single one of them was a false positive. Every. Single. One. That's hours of wasted time right there.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Yeah, that's — okay, I hear you, that's a significant operational impact. Michelle, do you know what categories of alerts were triggering? Like was it lateral movement, unusual login patterns, data exfiltration flags, or...?

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: It was mostly the behavioral anomaly ones. Uh, specifically around process execution — we were getting flagged for what looked like, I think the alert type was something like 'suspicious child process spawning' or something along those lines.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Okay. And those endpoints — are those your POS systems, back-office servers, or a mix?

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: Mix, but predominantly back-office. Our inventory management system runs a lot of scheduled jobs on Saturday mornings — restocking workflows, that kind of thing. We've never had issues with those being flagged before.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Right, right. So the patch may have reset or recalibrated the behavioral baseline thresholds. That's... yeah, that's a known issue we've been tracking with 4.2.1 actually. The patch included updates to the behavioral detection engine and in some environments it's basically starting the learning period over from scratch.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: I'm sorry — it's a known issue? Why weren't we notified before we applied it?

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: That's a fair point, Dominic, and I'm not going to make excuses for that. The advisory did go out but I think it was posted to the release notes portal and honestly that's not sufficient for an issue of this scope. I'll be flagging that internally.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: We shouldn't have to check a portal every time we apply a patch to find out if it's going to break our alerting. We have an account manager, we have a support contract — this should be proactive communication.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: You're absolutely right. And I will make sure that feedback gets escalated after this call. Right now though I want to focus on getting you fixed up — can we do that?

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: Yeah, okay. Yes. What do we do?

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: So there are two things we can do. First, I can push a configuration update to manually restore your previous baseline snapshot — we keep a 30-day history of your tuning profiles, so we can roll back to where you were before the patch. That should stop the flood of false positives within a couple hours of applying it.

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: Okay, that sounds good. And what's the second thing?

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: The second thing is we need to get you on a supervised re-learning period so the engine actually learns your environment properly after the patch — rather than just resetting blind. I'd want to work with you to set some suppression rules for those Saturday batch jobs specifically, so they don't trigger during the relearning window.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: How long does the relearning period take? Because we can't have our team babysitting alerts for weeks.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Typically seven to fourteen days for a retail environment with your endpoint count. But with the suppression rules in place and the baseline restore, you should be back to normal alert volumes pretty much immediately — the relearning just happens in the background.

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: Okay. And this won't affect our actual threat detection coverage during that window? Because — I mean, we can't just be blind to real threats either.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: No, the signature-based detection stays fully active throughout. The behavioral engine is supplementary — it catches things signatures might miss, but you're not losing your core detection capability. The suppression rules will be scoped really narrowly to those specific process patterns.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: Okay. That's — yeah, that's somewhat reassuring. Though I want to say, this is all happening, what, a week after we already had issues with Detect? The outage earlier this month?

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: I know. I know, and I won't pretend that's not a rough stretch. We take that seriously.

speaker: Dominic Flores
sentence: We're evaluating other vendors at renewal time, Sarah. I want to be transparent about that. This isn't a threat, it's just — we need to be confident in this platform and right now our confidence is shaken.

speaker: Sarah Chen

speaker: Michelle Tan

speaker: Michelle Tan

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: That would be great actually, yeah. Give me just a moment to pull that up... okay, I've got your tenant, I can see the baseline history. Your last stable profile is from March 13th — does that sound right, before the patch?

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: Yeah, we applied the patch on the 14th. So March 13th would be clean.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: Perfect. Initiating the restore now to the back-office agent group. You should start seeing alert volumes normalize within the next hour or two. I'll send you a follow-up email with the suppression rule recommendations and a write-up of what happened — something you can share with your team.

speaker: Dominic Flores

speaker: Sarah Chen

speaker: Michelle Tan
sentence: No, I think that's it for now. We'll keep an eye on the alert volumes. Thanks Sarah — I don't want it to sound like we're not appreciating the help today, we just... we need things to be more stable.

speaker: Sarah Chen
sentence: I hear you, Michelle. Thanks for your patience. I'll get that email over to you within the hour.
